message.content: we are workign on goraic so work in that folder and just reference the others as needed.  Understand this project whose requirements are stale.  read /external/go-llms to understand a new library we can use to build an agent. Understand taht project too, and then update goraic/REQUIREMENTS.md based on the new info you learn from go-llms

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	# goraic — Requirements (v0.2)
2	
3	This is the authoritative requirements document for `goraic`, the gorai coding agent.
4	It supersedes the prior `agent.json` configuration and recasts the agent as a mesh of
5	**named services on NATS**, configured by a **Robot Definition Language (RDL)** file
6	that is structurally identical to gorai's RDL (see `/external/gorai/pkg/config`).
7	
8	[VISION.md](VISION.md) is the architectural intent. This file is the contract that
9	[docs/DESIGN.md](docs/DESIGN.md) and [PLAN.md](PLAN.md) must satisfy. Where this file
10	and DESIGN.md disagree, **this file wins** — fix DESIGN.md.
11	
12	The shorthand binary name is `goraic` (`cmd/goraic`).
13	
14	---
15	
16	## 1. Purpose and scope
17	
18	`goraic` is an open-source coding agent that:
19	
20	1. Runs as a single binary with an embedded NATS server, OR connects to an external
21	   NATS server shared with other `goraic` and `gorai` processes.
22	2. Treats every internal component — LLM adapters, capability tools, coordinators,
23	   role agents — as a **service on the NATS bus**, addressed by name.
24	3. Drives the user experience through a **TUI** (scrollback-streaming, pinned status
25	   line). All internal traffic goes to a log file; never to stdout.
26	4. Replaces hardcoded roles (planner/coder/reviewer) with **user-defined slash-command
27	   services**: `/plan`, `/code`, `/code-review`, `/write`, `/test`, `/design`, etc.
28	   Each slash command is a named service in the RDL, bound to a specific LLM service
29	   (and therefore a specific model).
30	5. Runs slash-command invocations **concurrently** in goroutines so the user can queue
31	   the next command without waiting for the previous to finish, and so commands can be
32	   chained into pipelines that span multiple LLMs and multiple machines.
33	
34	Non-goals (v0.2): web UI; persistent multi-user server; replacing Claude Code as a
35	hosted product; robot control (handled by gorai itself — `goraic` shares the same
36	bus protocol so robot services and coding services are reachable from each other).
37	
38	---
39	
40	## 2. Architectural commitments
41	
42	These are the load-bearing decisions; everything else derives from them.
43	
44	**REQ-ARCH-1 — Single binary, mesh internally.** `goraic` ships as one executable. By
45	default it starts an embedded NATS server (`github.com/gorai/gorai/pkg/embeddednats`).
46	All internal services — LLM adapters, capability services, command services,
47	coordinators — are goroutines that talk over NATS, never via direct Go calls.
48	
49	**REQ-ARCH-2 — gorai-NATS is the only contract.** Cross-service interactions use the
50	gorai-NATS wire protocol (`TaskRequest`, `TaskResult`, `ToolDefinition`, `ToolCall`,
51	`ToolCallRequest`, `ToolCallResponse`, `ProgressEvent`, `StatusMessage`). JSON on the
52	wire; schemas committed under `docs/protocol/` and validated in tests.
53	
54	**REQ-ARCH-3 — Local vs external services are explicit.** A service is one of:
55	
56	- **Local** — compiled into the `goraic` binary; runs as a goroutine on the embedded
57	  NATS server. The default for all services in the stock build.
58	- **External (child process)** — declared with `external.enabled = true` and an
59	  absolute `command`. The `goraic` runtime spawns the process and passes
60	  `NATS_URL` so the child connects to the embedded (or configured) bus. Modeled
61	  exactly on gorai's external-service requirement (`REQ-EXTERNAL-SVC-*`).
62	- **External (remote, same bus)** — a service running in some *other* `goraic` or
63	  `gorai` process on the same NATS server. From the calling service's point of view
64	  it is just another subject prefix. Declared via a `remotes` block (optional) for
65	  discovery hints and by *not* declaring the service locally.
66	
67	**REQ-ARCH-4 — One NATS, many machines.** A `goraic` instance may be configured to
68	connect to an existing NATS URL instead of starting an embedded server. When two or
69	more `goraic` (or `gorai`) processes share a NATS server, every service published on
70	that bus is callable from every process. This is how work spreads across machines and
71	LLMs (e.g., Claude on a laptop, Ollama on a workstation, GPT on a CI box).
72	
73	**REQ-ARCH-5 — TUI front, file-only logs.** The TUI is the only writer to stdout. All
74	NATS traffic, LLM traces, service lifecycle, and errors go to the log file named in
75	the RDL `log` block. The single permitted stderr write is one fatal line at startup
76	if the log file cannot be opened.
77	
78	**REQ-ARCH-6 — Open-standard agent files.** `goraic` is built on two open
79	standards and **MUST** comply with both:
80	
81	- **Project agentic instructions MUST follow the AGENTS.md specification at
82	  https://agents.md/.** The repo-root `AGENTS.md` is the canonical place where a
83	  project tells `goraic` (and any other agents.md-compatible coding agent) how
84	  to build, test, lint, and behave inside the codebase. Nested `AGENTS.md`
85	  files in subdirectories override the root file for work under that subtree
86	  ("closest AGENTS.md to the edited file wins"), exactly as the spec requires.
87	- **Skills MUST follow the Agent Skills specification at
88	  https://agentskills.io/.** Every skill is a directory containing a `SKILL.md`
89	  with the spec-defined YAML frontmatter (`name`, `description`, and the
90	  optional `license`, `compatibility`, `metadata`, `allowed-tools` fields), and
91	  may include the optional `scripts/`, `references/`, and `assets/`
92	  subdirectories. Skills are loaded with the spec's three-stage **progressive
93	  disclosure** model.
94	
95	Skills attach to **command services**, not to LLM adapters. Compatibility with
96	Claude Code conventions (`CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/skills/`) is supported as a
97	fallback discovery source only; it is **not** a substitute for spec compliance.
98	A skill that loads from `.claude/skills/` is still validated against
99	agentskills.io and rejected if non-conformant.
100	
101	---
102	
103	## 3. The Robot Definition Language (RDL)
104	
105	`agent.json` is removed. Configuration uses an RDL file structurally identical to
106	gorai's RDL v2 (see `/external/gorai/pkg/config/config.go`, `RDL`, `ServiceConfig`,
107	`LogConfig`, `RemoteConfig`).
108	
109	**REQ-RDL-1 — File and discovery.** Default config path is `./robot.json`. CLI flag
110	`--config <path>` overrides. If neither is present `goraic` exits with a clear error
111	naming the expected path.
112	
113	**REQ-RDL-2 — Wire-compatible types (not Go-type identity).** goraic's config
114	loader defines its own Go types whose JSON shape **MUST match gorai's
115	`pkg/config` types** for the fields they share (`version`, `robot`, `log`,
116	`nats`, `services[].name|type|model|disabled|attributes|depends_on|external`,
117	`remotes`). goraic extends the format with three additions: a top-level
118	`defaults` block (required `command` field), and two new `services[].type`
119	values (`command`, `prompt-log`). A gorai parser reading a goraic RDL ignores
120	those additions; a goraic parser reading a gorai RDL works as long as
121	`defaults.command` is satisfiable. A test asserts JSON-shape compatibility
122	against a representative gorai-style RDL. The robot-section fields
123	(`robot.name`, `robot.description`) are required by the schema but carry no
124	operational meaning for a coding agent; the name appears in logs and status messages.
125	**Rationale:** binding directly to gorai's Go types couples goraic's release
126	cadence to gorai's. The wire is the contract.
127	
128	**REQ-RDL-3 — Service types recognised by goraic.** The `services[].type` enum is
129	small and closed. Connection-to-an-LLM is a named service so that several
130	commands can share one endpoint+credential configuration and an operator has a
131	single place to rotate a key or repoint at a different Ollama host.
132	
133	| `type`        | Purpose                                                       |
134	|---------------|---------------------------------------------------------------|
135	| `llm`         | A configured LLM endpoint (protocol, endpoint URL, API-key env var, default model). Local to the binary; referenced by name from command services. |
136	| `command`     | A slash-command role (`/plan`, `/code`, ...). The user-visible unit of work. Binds to an `llm` service by name. |
137	| `coordinator` | Multi-step orchestrator that composes command services        |
138	| `capability`  | Tool host (files, search, shell, repo, memory) — auto-registered; entries only present to disable or override |
139	| `prompt-log`  | Subscribes to all task traffic and writes per-session JSONL   |
140	
141	All names are NATS-subject-safe (`^[a-z][a-z0-9_-]*$`). Duplicate names are rejected.
142	
143	**Rationale for `llm` as a first-class service type:** v0.1 hid the LLM behind a
144	Go interface inside the agent; an interim cut of v0.2 inlined `provider`/`model`
145	into each command's `attributes`. The inline form duplicates endpoint and
146	credential config any time two commands point at the same provider, which is
147	the common case (a typical RDL has several Anthropic-backed commands). Making
148	`llm` a named service collapses that duplication, gives `/services` a real
149	target to enumerate, and provides one validation point for "is `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`
150	set?" at startup. Cross-machine load spreading still works (REQ-DIST-2) — the
151	*command* moves to the other machine, and the LLM service it depends on either
152	moves with it or is reached over the bus by name.
153	
154	**REQ-RDL-4 — External services use gorai's shape.** When `services[].external` is set
155	the runtime spawns the named command and passes `NATS_URL` in the environment, per
156	`REQ-EXTERNAL-SVC-1..3` from gorai. Restart policy honours the gorai
157	`external.restart` field.
158	
159	**REQ-RDL-5 — Remote NATS support.** A top-level `nats` block selects the bus:
160	
161	```json
162	"nats": { "embedded": true }                         // default
163	"nats": { "embedded": false, "url": "nats://host:4222" }   // join shared bus
164	```
165	
166	When `embedded` is false, the `goraic` instance does NOT start a server; it connects
167	as a client. All `services` declared in *this* RDL are then published onto that
168	shared bus, and services declared elsewhere (by other `goraic` or `gorai`
169	processes on the same bus) are discoverable by their NATS subject prefix.
170	
171	**REQ-RDL-6 — Minimal RDL.**
172	
173	```json
174	{
175	  "version": "2",
176	  "robot": { "name": "goraic-local" }
177	}
178	```
179	
180	This starts the embedded NATS server, no other services. The CLI is then useless
181	(no command services), but startup must succeed and exit cleanly.
182	
183	**REQ-RDL-7 — Representative RDL.** See §10 for a full example.
184	
185	---
186	
187	## 4. Services
188	
189	### 4.1 Local vs external
190	
191	**REQ-SVC-1.** A service is local when its `type` is implemented inside the goraic
192	binary and `external` is absent. The runtime constructs it via a registered factory
193	and runs it as a goroutine.
194	
195	**REQ-SVC-2.** A service is external when `external.enabled = true`. The runtime
196	must NOT instantiate it in-process. It must spawn the named command and forward
197	`NATS_URL`. Cross-service calls are unchanged — they go over NATS.
198	
199	**REQ-SVC-3.** A service is remote (no declaration needed in the local RDL) when it
200	exists on the shared NATS bus from another process. Calling code uses its name as a
201	subject prefix and the call works without local config, provided the caller's RDL
202	opts into the shared bus per REQ-RDL-5.
203	
204	### 4.2 Heartbeats and discovery
205	
206	**REQ-SVC-4.** Every service publishes `StatusMessage` heartbeats on `<name>.status`
207	with its type, model (if applicable), and the list of tools it exposes. This is the
208	basis for the `/services` user-facing command (§7.4) and for tool discovery by
209	command services.
210	
211	### 4.3 LLM services (in goraic) and provider library (`../gorai-llm-service`)
212	
213	An LLM is a **named service** declared in the RDL (`type: "llm"`). It is local
214	to the binary — its NATS-service wrapper compiles into `goraic` — but the
215	per-protocol translation lives in the sibling `gorai-llm-service` library.
216	goraic's `internal/llmsvc` package is a single generic wrapper that takes any
217	`gorai-llm-service` provider and exposes it on the bus as a service of type
218	`llm`.
219	
220	**REQ-LLM-SVC-1 — RDL shape.** A service of `type: "llm"` declares the
221	following `attributes`:
222	
223	| Attribute       | Required | Purpose                                                                                                                                              |
224	|-----------------|----------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
225	| `protocol`      | yes      | Wire format. Enum: `anthropic \| openai \| ollama \| gemini \| mock \| remote`. Selects the translator compiled into the binary from `gorai-llm-service`. Use `remote` when inference runs on a separate host via `goraci-llm-svc` (see REQ-LLM-SVC-6). |
226	| `endpoint`      | sometimes | Base URL. Optional (SaaS protocols default to their official URL; `ollama` defaults to `http://localhost:11434`). |
227	| `api_key_env`   | optional | Name of an env var holding the API key. **Never the key itself.** Defaults per protocol (`ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, `OPENAI_API_KEY`, `GEMINI_API_KEY`); ignored for `ollama`/`mock`. |
228	| `headers_env`   | optional | Map of `header-name → env-var-name` for proxies/gateways that need extra headers. Values resolved from env at startup. |
229	| `model`         | optional | Default model for callers that don't specify one. Command services may override.                                                                     |
230	| `temperature`   | optional | Default temperature for callers that don't specify one.                                                                                              |
231	| `max_tokens`    | optional | Default per-request max tokens.                                                                                                                      |
232	| `timeout_seconds` | optional | Per-request timeout.                                                                                                                               |
233	| `tls`           | optional | `{ ca_cert_file, insecure_skip_verify }` for private CAs. `insecure_skip_verify: true` produces a startup warning.                                   |
234	
235	**REQ-LLM-SVC-2 — Credentials are env-only.** The RDL schema **forbids** any
236	field whose name matches `/api[_-]?key$/` from carrying a literal value; the
237	only accepted shape is `api_key_env: <ENV_VAR_NAME>`. A loader test asserts
238	the schema rejects a literal `api_key`. There is no inline-secret escape hatch.
239	
240	**REQ-LLM-SVC-3 — Startup fail-fast.** Every declared LLM service resolves its
241	`api_key_env` (and any `headers_env` values) at startup. If the protocol
242	requires credentials and the named env var is unset or empty, `goraic` exits
243	with a non-zero status and a clear error naming the LLM service and the
244	missing env var. There is no lazy first-request failure.
245	
246	**REQ-LLM-SVC-4 — Endpoint defaults are compiled-in per protocol.** The RDL
247	stays minimal for the common SaaS case (declaring `protocol` + `model` is
248	enough). Operators only set `endpoint` to override a default or to point at a
249	private host.
250	
251	**REQ-LLM-SVC-5 — Local NATS exposure.** The runtime publishes each `llm`
252	service on its own subject prefix derived from `name` (per existing gorai-NATS
253	conventions). Command services dispatch completion requests to that subject.
254	Heartbeats are emitted under `<name>.status` per REQ-SVC-4. Per REQ-DIST-2,
255	an LLM service running on another node in the same NATS bus is reachable from
256	remote commands without any extra config in the caller's RDL beyond a
257	reference name.
258	
259	**REQ-LLM-SVC-6 — Remote protocol is a no-op local service.** When
260	`attributes.protocol = "remote"`, `llmsvc.New` returns a `remoteService`
261	that satisfies `bus.Service` but has an empty `Start()` — it subscribes to
262	nothing and emits no heartbeats. The actual subscriber is `goraic-llm-svc`
263	running on the remote host. This prevents the dual-subscriber fan-out problem
264	that would occur if goraic's in-process llmsvc and the remote daemon both
265	subscribed to `<name>.request` on a plain (non-queue) subject.
266	
267	The remote daemon MUST use a NATS queue group (`QueueSubscribe`) so that
268	multiple daemon instances on the same subject load-balance rather than fan-out.
269	
270	Credential resolution (`REQ-LLM-SVC-3`) is skipped entirely for
271	`protocol: "remote"` — no provider is constructed, no env vars are read.
272	
273	**REQ-LLM-1 — gorai-llm-service is a library, not a binary.**
274	`github.com/emergingrobotics/gorai-llm-service`, sibling checkout at
275	`../gorai-llm-service`. The `goraic` `go.mod` adds
276	`replace github.com/emergingrobotics/gorai-llm-service => ../gorai-llm-service`.
277	There is no `cmd/` in this module; no NATS code; no service plumbing. The
278	single responsibility of the module is to provide per-protocol translators
279	that goraic's LLM-service wrapper consumes.
280	
281	**REQ-LLM-2 — Common interface, per-provider package.** Layout:
282	
283	```
284	gorai-llm-service/
285	  pkg/llm/                 Provider interface + canonical Request/Response types
286	  pkg/types/               Canonical TaskRequest/TaskResult re-exported for callers
287	  providers/anthropic/     Anthropic Messages API translation
288	  providers/openai/        OpenAI / chat-completions translation
289	  providers/ollama/        Ollama (OpenAI-compat) + qwen degradation defender
290	    providers/mock/          scripted adapter for deterministic E2E tests
291	```
292	
293	Each provider exports a constructor `New(cfg llm.Config) (llm.Provider, error)`
294	that returns an object implementing `Complete(ctx context.Context, req
295	llm.Request) (llm.Response, error)`.
296	
297	**REQ-LLM-3 — Canonical Response at the library boundary.** Callers (the
298	goraic LLM-service wrapper) see only the canonical types from `pkg/llm`.
299	Provider-specific quirks (Anthropic content blocks, Ollama tool-call-as-text
300	degradation, OpenAI function-vs-tool naming) are handled inside the provider
301	package.
302	
303	**REQ-LLM-4 — goraic stops embedding provider SDKs directly.** After the
304	extraction, `goraic/internal/llm/{anthropic,ollama,mock}` is deleted. The
305	replacement is a single `goraic/internal/llmsvc` package: a thin
306	NATS-service wrapper that takes any `llm.Provider` and exposes it as an LLM
307	service on the bus. Provider-specific dependencies (`anthropic-sdk-go`,
308	`go-openai`) move into their respective provider packages inside
309	`gorai-llm-service`. After the move, `go.mod` in `goraic` lists no
310	provider SDK directly.
311	
312	**REQ-LLM-5 — Shared providers (in-process cache).** When two `llm` services
313	in the same RDL declare the same `(protocol, endpoint, api_key_env)` tuple,
314	the runtime SHOULD share a single underlying provider client to avoid
315	duplicate connection setup and to coalesce rate-limit budgeting. Two `llm`
316	services that differ only in `model` are a legitimate "two LLMs, one
317	endpoint" pattern and share an underlying client.
318	
319	### 4.4 Capability services (auto-registered)
320	
321	The five capability services — `files`, `search`, `shell`, `repo`, `memory` — are
322	compiled into the goraic binary and **auto-register at startup**. They are NOT
323	listed in a typical `robot.json`. The RDL only carries a `type: "capability"`
324	entry when the operator wants to disable one or override its attributes.
325	
326	**REQ-CAP-1 — Closed set.** The known capability set is fixed:
327	`{files, search, shell, repo, memory}`. The validator rejects a
328	`type: "capability"` entry whose name is not in this set (catches typos like
329	`shel` that would otherwise silently no-op).
330	
331	**REQ-CAP-2 — Disable.** An RDL entry with `disabled: true` causes the runtime
332	to skip the capability's factory; the service does not start and does not
333	publish heartbeats. `--safe` continues to work as a CLI alias for
334	`shell.disabled=true`.
335	
336	**REQ-CAP-3 — Per-capability overrides via `attributes`.** A user MAY add an
337	entry with `attributes: {...}` to override defaults (e.g.,
338	`{"name":"shell","type":"capability","attributes":{"allowlist":["ls","cat"]}}`).
339	The runtime passes the attributes to the factory; factories that don't yet
340	honor a given attribute log a warning and use the default. CLI flags
341	(`--safe`, `--allow-outside-working-dir`) remain the path for boot-time
342	overrides; the RDL is the path for per-environment policy.
343	
344	**REQ-CAP-4 — No auto-discovery for LLMs (explicit policy).** Capability
345	services auto-register because they have no per-user configuration — every
346	goraic ships with the same `files` service. LLMs are the opposite: choosing
347	which LLM to use is a deliberate operator decision involving API keys, costs,
348	model quality, and data egress. goraic **does NOT** auto-detect an Ollama at
349	`localhost:11434`, **does NOT** auto-pick a provider when `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`
350	is set, etc. Every LLM is declared explicitly in the RDL as a `type: "llm"`
351	service (REQ-LLM-SVC-1).
352	
353	### 4.5 Prompt-log service (from enhancements.md)
354	
355	**REQ-SVC-5 — Prompt logging.** A service of type `prompt-log` subscribes to every
356	`*.task` and `*.result` subject and writes each as a JSON line to
357	`./.goraic/sessions/<YYYY-MM-DD>.jsonl` in the working directory. The file's schema
358	is the gorai-NATS envelope; replay is `nats pub` of the captured lines. The
359	`.goraic/` folder is auto-created and added to `.gitignore` on first write.
360	
361	---
362	
363	## 5. Slash-command services (the central new concept)
364	
365	This is the load-bearing change vs v0.1. **The user does not talk to "the agent"; the
366	user talks to a chosen command service by typing its name as a slash command.** The
367	RDL decides which model is behind each command.
368	
369	### 5.1 Mapping
370	
371	**REQ-CMD-1 — A command service is a named service of `type: "command"`.** Its `name`
372	field is exactly the slash-command token the user will type (without the leading
373	slash). So `name: "plan"` is invoked as `/plan ...`, `name: "code-review"` as
374	`/code-review ...`, and so on.
375	
376	**REQ-CMD-2 — A command service references a declared LLM service.** The
377	command's `attributes` name an existing `llm` service rather than inlining
378	provider/endpoint/credential config:
379	
380	- `llm` (required) — the `name` of a service of `type: "llm"` declared elsewhere
381	  in this RDL or reachable on the bus per REQ-DIST-2.
382	
383	Optional attributes that override the LLM service's defaults for invocations
384	made by this command only:
385	
386	- `model` — overrides `attributes.model` on the LLM service (e.g., the LLM
387	  service defaults to `claude-sonnet-4-6`, but `/explain` uses
388	  `claude-haiku-4-5`).
389	- `temperature` — overrides the LLM service's `temperature`.
390	- `max_tokens` — overrides the LLM service's `max_tokens`.
391	
392	The inline `provider`/`api_key_env`/`base_url` attributes from earlier drafts
393	are **not accepted**. A command service that declares any of those is a fatal
394	RDL validation error with a migration message pointing at this section.
395	Mapping one slash command to multiple models is still out of scope for v0.2 —
396	declare a second command instead (e.g., `code-cloud` and `code-local`).
397	
398	**REQ-CMD-3 — Optional skills and permissions.** A command service may declare:
399	
400	- `attributes.skills`: a list of skill filenames or skill IDs to load into the
401	  system prompt for this command.
402	- `attributes.permissions`: one of `readonly | standard | broad` — gates the tools
403	  exposed to this command via the capability services.
404	- `attributes.system_prompt`: an optional inline system prompt override; if absent
405	  a default is composed from the command name and loaded skills.
406	
407	**REQ-CMD-4 — Default command set.** The shipped example RDL declares, at minimum,
408	these command services: `/plan`, `/design`, `/code`, `/code-review`, `/test`,
409	`/write`, `/explain`. None of these names are hardcoded in goraic; they exist
410	only because they appear in the RDL. The user is free to add `/refactor`,
411	`/security-audit`, `/translate`, etc., by adding entries.
412	
413	### 5.2 Invocation
414	
415	**REQ-CMD-5 — Chat input parsing.** The TUI input parser:
416	
417	1. Trims leading whitespace.
418	2. If the line begins with `/`, the token up to the next whitespace is the command
419	   name; the rest is the **prompt**.
420	3. If the command name resolves to a built-in (`/exit`, `/quit`, `/clear`,
421	   `/help`, `/services`, `/cancel`, see §7), it runs the built-in.
422	4. Otherwise the parser looks up the command service by name. If found, it
423	   dispatches a `TaskRequest` to that command service on `<name>.task`. If not
424	   found, it prints an error listing the available commands.
425	
426	**REQ-CMD-6 — Bare-prompt default (mandatory).** The RDL **must** set
427	`defaults.command` to the name of one of the declared `command` services. If a
428	chat input line does not begin with `/`, it is dispatched to that command
429	service. A missing `defaults.command`, an unknown name, or a name whose target
430	is not of `type: "command"` is a **fatal RDL validation error** at startup —
431	`goraic` refuses to launch with a clear message naming the offending field. This
432	makes the implicit-target behaviour explicit (no surprise "which model just
433	answered me?") and forces every RDL to declare the operator's intent for bare
434	prompts. There is no implicit "the agent."
435	
436	**REQ-CMD-7 — The command service is the agent.** A command service IS a ReAct
437	agent service. It receives `TaskRequest`, runs a reasoning loop against its bound
438	LLM, discovers tools from `*.status` heartbeats, dispatches tool calls to
439	capability services, and returns a `TaskResult`. The existing `internal/agent`
440	package becomes the implementation of command services (renamed accordingly).
441	
442	### 5.3 Concurrency and chaining
443	
444	**REQ-CMD-8 — Each invocation runs in its own goroutine.** Submitting a slash
445	command does NOT block the prompt. The TUI returns to the input line immediately;
446	progress and final output for the invocation stream into the scrollback as they
447	arrive.
448	
449	**REQ-CMD-9 — Multiple commands in flight.** The user may submit several slash
450	commands back to back. The status line shows one entry per active task with its
451	command name, elapsed time, and current action. The scrollback preserves output
452	ordering by completion; partial / interleaved output is allowed but each task's
453	lines are prefixed with the task id (short suffix) so the user can disambiguate.
454	There is no global queue; tasks run as soon as they are dispatched.
455	
456	**REQ-CMD-10 — Cancellation.** `/cancel <task-id>` cancels one task. `/cancel all`
457	cancels every in-flight task. Ctrl-C while typing cancels the most recent task
458	and confirms exit only on a second Ctrl-C at an empty prompt (existing behaviour
459	preserved for the single-task case).
460	
461	**REQ-CMD-11 — Chaining (pipe syntax).** The chat parser recognises a single-line
462	pipeline:
463	
464	```
465	/plan write PLAN.md from DESIGN.md | /code-review review PLAN.md | /code implement phase 1 of PLAN.md
466	```
467	
468	Each `|` separates one slash-command invocation. Pipelines run sequentially: the
469	next stage starts when the previous stage's `TaskResult.Status == complete`. The
470	prior stage's `FinalAnswer` is prepended to the next stage's prompt as
471	`PriorContext` (existing protocol field). A failing stage stops the pipeline and
472	prints the failure. The user prompt returns immediately after dispatch — the
473	pipeline runs in its own goroutine like a single command.
474	
475	**REQ-CMD-12 — Parallel branches (optional, deferred).** A future extension is
476	`&&` for sequential AND, `&` for parallel branches. **Out of scope for v0.2 unless
477	trivially derivable from REQ-CMD-11.** The grammar in v0.2 is restricted to the
478	pipe.
479	
480	**REQ-CMD-13 — Pipelines span machines.** Because each stage is a NATS dispatch by
481	name, a pipeline transparently spans machines as soon as the corresponding command
482	services are running on remote `goraic` instances connected to the same NATS bus.
483	
484	### 5.4 Worked example
485	
486	```
487	/plan write a file PLAN.md that lays out implementation phases for the project in DESIGN.md
488	```
489	
490	1. Parser splits `plan` and the prompt.
491	2. Looks up command service `plan` in the RDL. Finds it bound (via
492	   `attributes.llm`) to the LLM service `anthropic-sonnet` (also declared in
493	   the RDL, with `attributes.protocol: "anthropic"` and
494	   `attributes.model: "claude-sonnet-4-6"`).
495	3. Dispatches `TaskRequest{Prompt: "write a file...", ...}` to `plan.task`.
496	4. The `plan` command service receives it, builds messages (system prompt +
497	   skills + user prompt), sends a completion request to `anthropic-sonnet.request`,
498	   handles tool calls against `files.tools.write_file`, etc., and publishes the
499	   final `TaskResult` on `plan.result`.
500	5. The TUI surfaces progress and the final answer into the scrollback as it
501	   arrives.
502	6. The user can type `/code-review review PLAN.md` immediately — it dispatches in
503	   parallel; results interleave; status line shows two active tasks.
504	
505	---
506	
507	## 6. Distributed execution
508	
509	**REQ-DIST-1 — A `goraic` instance must be runnable in one of three NATS modes.**
510	
511	| Mode               | RDL `nats` block                                               | Use                                                  |
512	|--------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
513	| Embedded (default) | `{ "embedded": true }` or omitted                              | Solo laptop use                                      |
514	| Shared external    | `{ "embedded": false, "url": "nats://host:4222" }`             | Multiple `goraic`/`gorai` processes on a shared bus  |
515	| Embedded + listen  | `{ "embedded": true, "listen": ":4222" }`                      | One `goraic` acts as the bus; others connect to it   |
516	
517	**REQ-DIST-2 — Mesh authority is the bus.** Whether a command or LLM service is
518	local, external-child-process, or running on another machine is invisible to
519	the caller. The only thing the caller does is publish to `<name>.task` (for
520	commands) or `<llm>.request` (for LLMs). This makes heterogeneous fleets — a
521	laptop with `/plan` bound to a Claude LLM service on the laptop, a workstation
522	with a beefy-Ollama LLM service that several commands across the bus reference,
523	a CI box with `/code-review` bound to a cheaper cloud LLM — work without any
524	code change on the caller side. Operator runs the right services on the right
525	machines; each command names the LLM service it depends on.
526	
527	**REQ-DIST-3 — LLM services are addressable by name across the bus.** A
528	command's `attributes.llm` value may resolve to a service declared in this
529	RDL or to one declared on another goraic instance reachable via the shared
530	NATS bus (REQ-RDL-5). The RDL validator MAY accept a reference to a name not
531	declared locally provided either (a) `nats.embedded` is false, or (b) the
532	`remotes` block names the LLM service explicitly; otherwise an unknown
533	`attributes.llm` is a fatal RDL validation error. To dedicate a machine to
534	hosting one or more LLM services, run a goraic instance there whose RDL
535	declares only `type: "llm"` services.
536	
537	**REQ-DIST-4 — No assumption of trust between processes.** When `goraic` connects
538	to an external NATS, credentials for that bus come from a `nats.credentials` field
539	or environment variable; no code path emits or logs credentials.
540	
541	---
542	
543	## 7. User interface
544	
545	### 7.1 TUI shape (unchanged from v0.1)
546	
547	**REQ-UI-1.** Scrollback-streaming output. While a turn is in flight, an inline
548	status line (spinner + current action + in/out token counts + elapsed) is rendered
549	directly below the user prompt and updates in place via \r\x1b[K (no scroll
550	region, no alternate screen). The status line is cleared when the turn completes
551	and replaced by the final answer; the next goraic> prompt then appears below.
552	Real terminal copy-paste must continue to work in the transcript.
553	
554	**REQ-UI-2.** All internal output goes to the log file; nothing internal appears on
555	the TUI.
556	
557	### 7.2 Prompt and history (from enhancements.md)
558	
559	**REQ-UI-3.** The input line is read with `chzyer/readline`. Up/Down arrows scroll
560	through prompt history. History is loaded from `./.goraic/history` and appended
561	on every accepted input. History entries are editable in place before resubmit.
562	
563	### 7.3 Multi-task display
564	
565	**REQ-UI-4.** While ≥1 task is in flight the status line cycles through the active
566	tasks (or shows a compact stack if more than one) with command name, short task
567	id, current action, and elapsed seconds. The transcript above is prefixed per task
568	when more than one task is in flight to avoid interleaving confusion.
569	
570	### 7.4 Built-in commands
571	
572	`/exit`, `/quit` — leave the session (existing).
573	`/clear` — reset the running conversation history (the `PriorContext` thread of
574	the chat session). The next turn dispatches with an empty prior, so the
575	status-line token bracket and context-window percentage reset to 0 on the
576	next slash-command invocation. Rejected if a task is currently in flight.
577	`/help` — list available commands (built-ins + every `command` service from the
578	RDL, with their bound LLM).
579	`/services` — list every service currently heartbeating on the bus, grouped by
580	type (llm / command / capability / coordinator / prompt-log), with the model for
581	LLMs and the latest seen timestamp. Implementation polls the `*.status` subjects
582	already required by REQ-SVC-4. This satisfies the enhancements.md request.
583	`/cancel <id|all>` — cancel as in REQ-CMD-10.
584	`/history [N]` — print the last N prompts from `./.goraic/history`.
585	
586	Slash names listed above are reserved: a `command` service must not use any of
587	them. The RDL validator rejects reserved-name collisions.
588	
589	---
590	
591	## 8. Agent files, skills, permissions, security
592	
593	`goraic` is built on two open standards. Section 8.1 covers project agentic
594	instructions (the **AGENTS.md** standard at https://agents.md/). Section 8.2
595	covers skills (the **Agent Skills** standard at https://agentskills.io/).
596	Sections 8.3–8.4 cover the permission and security envelope that wraps both.
597	
598	### 8.1 Project agentic instructions — AGENTS.md (agents.md)
599	
600	**REQ-AGENTS-1 — AGENTS.md is the canonical project-instructions file.**
601	`goraic` MUST load `AGENTS.md` per the open specification at
602	https://agents.md/. The file is plain Markdown at the repository root; it
603	contains whatever project-overview, build, test, code-style, security, and
604	"do-not-touch" guidance the project author considers necessary. There are no
605	required headings or fields — `goraic` MUST NOT impose any schema beyond what
606	agents.md itself defines.
607	
608	**REQ-AGENTS-2 — Nearest-AGENTS.md-wins.** When a command service is about to
609	act on a file at path `P`, the loader walks from `dir(P)` up to the repo root
610	collecting every `AGENTS.md` encountered. The **nearest** (deepest) file
611	overrides shallower ones for any guidance that conflicts. This is the
612	agents.md precedence rule and is mandatory; it is what makes monorepos work.
613	
614	**REQ-AGENTS-3 — Repo-root AGENTS.md is the session baseline.** When the
615	session starts, or for any task that does not target a specific file (`/plan`,
616	`/design`, free-form chat), the repo-root `AGENTS.md` from the current working
617	directory tree is treated as the baseline. `goraic` MUST inject the relevant
618	`AGENTS.md` content into the command service's system prompt at task dispatch
619	time.
620	
621	**REQ-AGENTS-4 — User prompt overrides files.** Per the agents.md spec, an
622	explicit instruction in the user's prompt overrides anything written in
623	`AGENTS.md`. The system prompt MUST be structured so the user prompt is the
624	last and most authoritative slot.
625	
626	**REQ-AGENTS-5 — CLAUDE.md compatibility fallback.** If no `AGENTS.md` is
627	present at a given level of the tree but a `CLAUDE.md` is, `goraic` MAY load
628	`CLAUDE.md` as a fallback. `AGENTS.md`, if present, always wins. A nearby
629	`CLAUDE.md` never overrides a shallower `AGENTS.md`. Compatibility is a
630	convenience for repositories that have not yet adopted agents.md; it is not
631	the goraic-native path.
632	
633	**REQ-AGENTS-6 — Reload on change.** Between task invocations, the loader
634	re-stats the `AGENTS.md` files it last loaded and reloads any whose mtime
635	changed. Edits to `AGENTS.md` MUST take effect on the next task without
636	restarting `goraic`.
637	
638	### 8.2 Skills — agentskills.io
639	
640	**REQ-SKILL-1 — Skills MUST conform to the Agent Skills specification.**
641	Every skill `goraic` loads MUST be a directory containing a `SKILL.md` file in
642	the format defined at https://agentskills.io/specification. The YAML
643	frontmatter MUST include the required fields:
644	
645	- `name` — 1–64 chars, lowercase `[a-z0-9-]`, no leading/trailing or
646	  consecutive hyphens; MUST equal the parent directory name.
647	- `description` — 1–1024 chars, non-empty.
648	
649	The optional fields `license`, `compatibility`, `metadata`, and `allowed-tools`
650	MUST be accepted and surfaced to command services; unknown fields MUST NOT
651	cause load failures (forward-compatibility).
652	
653	**REQ-SKILL-2 — Optional resource subdirectories.** A skill folder MAY contain
654	`scripts/`, `references/`, and `assets/` subdirectories. `goraic` MUST NOT
655	auto-execute anything in `scripts/`; execution happens only when the command
656	service calls a capability service that invokes the script. `references/` and
657	`assets/` are static files the LLM may request through the file-read
658	capability.
659	
660	**REQ-SKILL-3 — Progressive disclosure (mandatory three stages).** Skill
661	loading MUST follow the agentskills.io progressive-disclosure model:
662	
663	1. **Discovery.** At session start, the loader scans every skill source
664	   (REQ-SKILL-4) and indexes `name` + `description` only. Only these two
665	   fields appear in the skill catalog presented to the LLM.
666	2. **Activation.** When a skill is named in a command service's
667	   `attributes.skills`, listed on a `/skill` invocation, or selected by the
668	   LLM from the catalog during a task, the full `SKILL.md` body is loaded
669	   into the command service's system prompt for that task.
670	3. **Execution.** Files under `scripts/`, `references/`, or `assets/` load
671	   only when the LLM explicitly references them via the file-read or
672	   shell-exec capability service.
673	
674	**REQ-SKILL-4 — Discovery sources and layering.** The loader searches the
675	following sources, lowest priority first; later sources override earlier ones
676	when a `name` collides:
677	
678	1. Built-in skills compiled into the `goraic` binary (`internal/skills/builtin`).
679	2. User-global skills: `~/.config/goraic/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (and
680	   `~/.goraic/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` for backward compatibility).
681	3. Project-local skills: `./.goraic/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`.
682	4. Claude-Code compatibility sources (fallback only):
683	   `~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` and `./.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`.
684	5. Paths named in a command service's `attributes.skill_paths`.
685	
686	Claude sources are read at low priority and every loaded skill — regardless of
687	source — MUST pass agentskills.io validation. A non-conformant file is
688	rejected with a logged error and excluded from the catalog; it MUST NOT make
689	the session fail.
690	
691	**REQ-SKILL-5 — Skills attach to command services, not LLMs.** A command
692	service's `attributes.skills` lists skill `name` values to pre-activate for
693	every task that command handles. A skill referenced in `attributes.skills`
694	that does not exist in any discovery source is a **fatal RDL validation
695	error** at startup, with a message naming the missing skill.
696	
697	**REQ-SKILL-6 — `allowed-tools` is advisory until enforced.** The
698	spec-defined `allowed-tools` field is parsed and stored on the loaded skill.
699	For v0.2 it is exposed to command services as metadata only; v0.3 may use it
700	to further restrict the capability services a command may call when this
701	skill is active. v0.2 MUST NOT silently grant tools beyond what the command
702	service's `permissions` mode already allows.
703	
704	**REQ-SKILL-7 — Validation CLI.** `goraic skills list` MUST print the active
705	skill catalog (name, description, source, source path). `goraic skills
706	validate [path]` MUST run agentskills.io conformance checks on a skill
707	directory and exit non-zero on any violation. Reuse of the upstream
708	`skills-ref validate` rules is acceptable.
709	
710	### 8.3 Permissions
711	
712	**REQ-PERM-1.** Permission modes (`readonly | standard | broad`) gate the
713	tools the command service is willing to invoke. Gating happens at the
714	**command service**, not at the capability service, because permissions are
715	per-role. The `allowed-tools` frontmatter on an active skill (REQ-SKILL-6) MAY
716	narrow this set further but MUST NOT widen it.
717	
718	### 8.4 Security
719	
720	**REQ-SEC-1.** No credentials in logs, ever. API keys come from the environment;
721	their names may be logged but their values must not.
722	
723	**REQ-SEC-2.** External services are processes, not containers (REQ-EXTERNAL-SVC-1
724	from gorai). Absolute paths only.
725	
726	**REQ-SEC-3.** No `net/http` import in the `goraic` binary outside the LLM provider
727	packages. (Existing rule, preserved by the security test.)
728	
729	**REQ-SEC-4.** Skill loading MUST audit `SKILL.md` files for hidden Unicode
730	threats (bidirectional override characters, zero-width characters,
731	homoglyph-prone confusables) before activation. A skill whose `SKILL.md`
732	contains a critical finding is **blocked** from activation and logged; the
733	session continues with that skill excluded. `goraic audit skills` MUST
734	support a `--strip` flag that rewrites offending files to remove dangerous
735	characters in place. (This is the pi-go audit pattern; we adopt it
736	verbatim because the agentskills.io spec does not address supply-chain risk.)
737	
738	---
739	
740	## 9. Migration from v0.1
741	
742	**REQ-MIG-1.** Loading `agent.json` is no longer supported. The CLI prints a clear
743	migration message if it finds `./agent.json` and no `./robot.json` is present,
744	including a sample minimal `robot.json` the user can copy.
745	
746	**REQ-MIG-2.** The `behavior` service type from v0.1 is **renamed to `command`**.
747	The semantics are richer (one LLM binding per command, slash-command exposure) but
748	existing RDL entries can be ported by changing `type: "behavior"` to
749	`type: "command"` and ensuring the `name` is the desired slash-command token.
750	
751	**REQ-MIG-3.** The hardcoded `planner / coder / reviewer` coordinator pipeline is
752	not deleted but is reframed as **one possible composition of command services**
753	(`/plan`, `/code`, `/code-review`). The `coordinator` service type remains for
754	when a single user prompt should fan out across a fixed sequence; otherwise the
755	user composes pipelines with the pipe syntax (REQ-CMD-11).
756	
757	**REQ-MIG-4.** `internal/llm/*` packages are deleted in favour of importing
758	`gorai-llm-service`. The `internal/agent` package is renamed conceptually to
759	"command service" (file-level rename is a code-side concern; the public surface is
760	the new RDL `type: "command"`).
761	
762	---
763	
764	## 10. Worked RDL example
765	
766	```json
767	{
768	  "version": "2",
769	  "robot": {
770	    "name": "goraic-dev",
771	    "description": "Local goraic with cloud + local LLMs and remote helpers"
772	  },
773	  "log": {
774	    "file": "~/.local/share/gorai/goraic.log",
775	    "level": "info",
776	    "max_size_mb": 64,
777	    "max_backups": 3
778	  },
779	  "nats": { "embedded": true, "listen": "127.0.0.1:4222" },
780	  "defaults": { "command": "code" },
781	
782	  "services": [
783	    { "name": "anthropic-sonnet", "type": "llm",
784	      "attributes": {
785	        "protocol": "anthropic",
786	        "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6"
787	      } },
788	
789	    { "name": "openai-gpt5", "type": "llm",
790	      "attributes": {
791	        "protocol": "openai",
792	        "model": "gpt-5"
793	      } },
794	
795	    { "name": "ollama-coder", "type": "llm",
796	      "attributes": {
797	        "protocol": "ollama",
798	        "endpoint": "http://localhost:11434",
799	        "model": "qwen3-coder:30b",
800	        "temperature": 0.2
801	      } },
802	
803	    { "name": "plan",        "type": "command",
804	      "attributes": {
805	        "llm": "anthropic-sonnet",
806	        "permissions": "read-only",
807	        "skills": ["writing-plans", "systematic-debugging"]
808	      } },
809	
810	    { "name": "design",      "type": "command",
811	      "attributes": {
812	        "llm": "anthropic-sonnet",
813	        "permissions": "read-only",
814	        "skills": ["architecture-first"]
815	      } },
816	
817	    { "name": "code",        "type": "command",
818	      "attributes": {
819	        "llm": "ollama-coder",
820	        "permissions": "standard",
821	        "skills": ["test-driven-development", "edge-case-discovery"]
822	      } },
823	
824	    { "name": "code-review", "type": "command",
825	      "attributes": {
826	        "llm": "openai-gpt5",
827	        "permissions": "read-only",
828	        "skills": ["code-review"]
829	      } },
830	
831	    { "name": "test",        "type": "command",
832	      "attributes": {
833	        "llm": "ollama-coder",
834	        "permissions": "standard",
835	        "skills": ["test-as-guardrails"]
836	      } },
837	
838	    { "name": "write",       "type": "command",
839	      "attributes": {
840	        "llm": "anthropic-sonnet",
841	        "permissions": "read-only",
842	        "skills": ["documentation"]
843	      } },
844	
845	    { "name": "explain",     "type": "command",
846	      "attributes": {
847	        "llm": "ollama-coder",
848	        "permissions": "read-only"
849	      } },
850	
851	    { "name": "prompt-log",  "type": "prompt-log",
852	      "attributes": { "dir": ".goraic/sessions" } }
853	  ],
854	
855	  "remotes": [
856	    { "name": "buildbox",
857	      "address": "nats://buildbox.lan:4222",
858	      "services": ["ollama-large", "code-large"] }
859	  ]
860	}
861	```
862	
863	Note how three LLM services (`anthropic-sonnet`, `openai-gpt5`,
864	`ollama-coder`) back seven commands — exactly the kind of fan-in the v0.2
865	inline form duplicated. A second machine `buildbox.lan` runs its own goraic
866	instance against the same NATS URL with additional LLM services (e.g.
867	`ollama-large` backed by a beefy GPU) and additional command services
868	(e.g. `code-large` bound to `ollama-large`). The laptop's chat dispatches
869	`/code-large` over NATS; the buildbox handles it transparently. Work spreads
870	across the bus either by moving command services to another node, or by
871	hosting LLM services on a dedicated node and pointing remote commands at
872	them by name.
873	
874	---
875	
876	## 11. The `gorai-llm-service` sibling repo
877	
878	**REQ-LLM-REPO-1 — Repository.** `github.com/emergingrobotics/gorai-llm-service`,
879	sibling checkout at `../gorai-llm-service`.
880	
881	**REQ-LLM-REPO-2 — Authored layout (library only, no binary).**
882	
883	```
884	../gorai-llm-service/
885	  go.mod
886	  Makefile                       targets: build, test, clean, run-tests
887	  README.md
888	  REQUIREMENTS.md                derived from this section
889	  pkg/
890	    llm/
891	      llm.go                     Provider interface; Config, Request, Response,
892	                                 Usage, ToolCall canonical types
893	      degradation.go             tool-call-as-text detector + counters
894	    types/
895	      types.go                   shared exports for callers that don't want pkg/llm
896	  providers/
897	    anthropic/
898	      provider.go                New(cfg) llm.Provider; Complete(ctx, req)
899	      translate.go               messages <-> protocol
900	      provider_test.go
901	    openai/
902	      provider.go
903	      translate.go
904	      provider_test.go
905	    ollama/
906	      provider.go                + OpenAI-compatible client
907	      degraded.go                model floor + qwen quirks
908	      provider_test.go
909	      provider.go
910	      provider_test.go
911	    mock/
912	      provider.go                scripted; deterministic E2E
913	```
914	
915	No NATS code in this module. No standalone binary. No `cmd/`. The module is
916	purely a provider-protocol library. The NATS-service wrapper for the RDL
917	`type: "llm"` lives in goraic (`internal/llmsvc`) per REQ-LLM-4.
918	
919	**REQ-LLM-REPO-3 — Single source of truth.** Every LLM provider-protocol
920	translation lives here. The `goraic` binary contains no provider SDK
921	imports and no per-protocol translation code after the extraction; it
922	constructs an `llm.Provider` from an LLM service's attributes via the
923	appropriate provider package and calls `Complete` in-process from the
924	NATS-service wrapper.
925	
926	**REQ-LLM-REPO-4 — Protocol type ownership (JSON-Schema-as-contract).** The
927	canonical wire format lives in the JSON Schemas under
928	`goraic/docs/protocol/*.schema.json`. Both repos define their own Go
929	structs that marshal to the schemas; **the wire is the contract, not the Go
930	type**. Each repo MUST run a schema-validation test that loads the shared
931	schemas (gorai-llm-service vendors them via build-time copy or by referencing
932	the goraic checkout in CI) and round-trips representative instances.
933	This keeps the two modules independent — Go's `internal/` rule does not allow
934	`gorai-llm-service` to import `goraic/internal/protocol` even with a
935	`replace` directive — and prevents type drift through the schema gate.
936	
937	---
938	
939	## 12. Acceptance criteria
940	
941	A v0.2 build of `goraic` is accepted when, against a representative RDL like §10:
942	
943	1. `goraic chat` (with no `--config`) errors clearly because `./robot.json` is
944	   missing.
945	1a. An RDL with no `defaults.command`, or with `defaults.command` pointing at a
946	    non-existent or non-`command` service, fails validation at startup with a
947	    clear message naming the field.
948	2. With the RDL above present, `goraic chat` starts; the TUI shows the prompt.
949	3. `/help` lists `plan, design, code, code-review, test, write, explain` plus
950	   built-ins.
951	4. `/services` lists every LLM, capability, and command service, with `last seen`
952	   timestamps that update in real time.
953	5. `/plan write PLAN.md from DESIGN.md` dispatches; the prompt returns
954	   immediately; the status line shows the running plan task; the file
955	   `PLAN.md` is written by the time the task completes.
956	6. Submitting `/code-review review PLAN.md` while `/plan` is still running runs
957	   both tasks concurrently; both completions appear in the scrollback.
958	7. The pipeline `/plan ... | /code ...` runs the two stages sequentially with
959	   `PriorContext` threaded.
960	8. Pointing `nats` at an external URL where another goraic instance runs an
961	   LLM service (e.g., `ollama-large`), then declaring a command bound to that
962	   LLM name, makes the command transparently use the remote LLM. The local
963	   machine never imports that LLM's SDK.
964	8a. A command service that declares the legacy inline `provider`/`api_key_env`/
965	    `base_url` attributes fails RDL validation with a clear message pointing
966	    at REQ-CMD-2 and naming the offending attribute. A command's
967	    `attributes.llm` that names an undeclared LLM service (and is not present
968	    via a `remotes` block) also fails validation with a clear message.
969	8b. An `llm` service whose `protocol` requires credentials and whose declared
970	    `api_key_env` is unset at startup causes `goraic` to exit non-zero with
971	    a message naming the service and the env var.
972	9. `goraic/internal/llm/{anthropic,ollama,mock}` no longer exists; replaced by
973	   `goraic/internal/llmsvc` (a single generic wrapper). The security test for
974	   "no `net/http` outside `/llm`" is replaced by a test that asserts no
975	   provider-SDK imports are present in the `goraic` binary (all such imports
976	   live in `gorai-llm-service`).
977	10. The build is one `make build` away; tests pass via `make test`; race detector
978	    is on for packages with concurrency; no test is skipped or stubbed.
979	
980	---
981	
982	## 13. Open questions (call out, do not silently decide)
983	
984	- **Pipeline error semantics:** does a `degraded` stage continue the pipeline or
985	  stop it? Default proposal: stop with a visible reason; user can override with
986	  `/option pipeline-on-degraded continue`.
987	- **Tool discovery across remotes:** does a local command service automatically
988	  see tools advertised by a remote capability service on the same bus? Default
989	  proposal: yes, since heartbeats are bus-wide, but mark behind a flag for v0.2
990	  to keep blast radius small.
991	- **Pipeline grammar beyond `|`:** out of scope for v0.2 unless a real workflow
992	  forces it. Revisit in v0.3 with concrete use cases.
993	- **Persistent multi-session memory:** the `memory` capability service exists,
994	  but persistence across `goraic` runs is not specified here. Treat as a v0.3
995	  topic.
996	
997	These are tagged here so DESIGN.md and PLAN.md can either resolve them or carry
998	them forward as TODOs — they must not be silently decided by the implementor.
999	
1000	---
1001	
1002	## 14. What this means for the rest of the docs
1003	
1004	- **DESIGN.md** — update the "Configuration" section to the RDL above, drop the
1005	  `agent.json` reference, add command services, add the `gorai-llm-service`
1006	  dependency, document the concurrency model for command dispatch, and add a
1007	  dedicated section describing how AGENTS.md (per https://agents.md/) and the
1008	  Agent Skills standard (per https://agentskills.io/) are loaded, layered, and
1009	  injected into command service system prompts.
1010	- **PLAN.md** — most phases survive, but Phases 5–7 (LLM adapters) and Phase 9
1011	  (Agent Service) are restructured around `gorai-llm-service` and command
1012	  services. Phase 12 (Coordinator) becomes optional. A new phase covers the
1013	  RDL migration (drop `agent.json`, adopt `pkg/config`) and a new phase covers
1014	  the chat parser + concurrency model.
1015	- **agent.json** — delete; replace with `robot.json` at the repo root as the
1016	  shipped example RDL.
1017	- **README.md** — currently a stub about pi computation; rewrite to describe
1018	  goraic, the RDL, and the slash-command workflow once the implementation lands.
1019	
1020	---
1021	
1022	*Source of truth for goraic. Update this file before changing the design; update
1023	the design before changing the implementation.*
1024

file.content:
# goraic — Requirements (v0.2)

This is the authoritative requirements document for `goraic`, the gorai coding agent.
It supersedes the prior `agent.json` configuration and recasts the agent as a mesh of
**named services on NATS**, configured by a **Robot Definition Language (RDL)** file
that is structurally identical to gorai's RDL (see `/external/gorai/pkg/config`).

[VISION.md](VISION.md) is the architectural intent. This file is the contract that
[docs/DESIGN.md](docs/DESIGN.md) and [PLAN.md](PLAN.md) must satisfy. Where this file
and DESIGN.md disagree, **this file wins** — fix DESIGN.md.

The shorthand binary name is `goraic` (`cmd/goraic`).

---

## 1. Purpose and scope

`goraic` is an open-source coding agent that:

1. Runs as a single binary with an embedded NATS server, OR connects to an external
   NATS server shared with other `goraic` and `gorai` processes.
2. Treats every internal component — LLM adapters, capability tools, coordinators,
   role agents — as a **service on the NATS bus**, addressed by name.
3. Drives the user experience through a **TUI** (scrollback-streaming, pinned status
   line). All internal traffic goes to a log file; never to stdout.
4. Replaces hardcoded roles (planner/coder/reviewer) with **user-defined slash-command
   services**: `/plan`, `/code`, `/code-review`, `/write`, `/test`, `/design`, etc.
   Each slash command is a named service in the RDL, bound to a specific LLM service
   (and therefore a specific model).
5. Runs slash-command invocations **concurrently** in goroutines so the user can queue
   the next command without waiting for the previous to finish, and so commands can be
   chained into pipelines that span multiple LLMs and multiple machines.

Non-goals (v0.2): web UI; persistent multi-user server; replacing Claude Code as a
hosted product; robot control (handled by gorai itself — `goraic` shares the same
bus protocol so robot services and coding services are reachable from each other).

---

## 2. Architectural commitments

These are the load-bearing decisions; everything else derives from them.

**REQ-ARCH-1 — Single binary, mesh internally.** `goraic` ships as one executable. By
default it starts an embedded NATS server (`github.com/gorai/gorai/pkg/embeddednats`).
All internal services — LLM adapters, capability services, command services,
coordinators — are goroutines that talk over NATS, never via direct Go calls.

**REQ-ARCH-2 — gorai-NATS is the only contract.** Cross-service interactions use the
gorai-NATS wire protocol (`TaskRequest`, `TaskResult`, `ToolDefinition`, `ToolCall`,
`ToolCallRequest`, `ToolCallResponse`, `ProgressEvent`, `StatusMessage`). JSON on the
wire; schemas committed under `docs/protocol/` and validated in tests.

**REQ-ARCH-3 — Local vs external services are explicit.** A service is one of:

- **Local** — compiled into the `goraic` binary; runs as a goroutine on the embedded
  NATS server. The default for all services in the stock build.
- **External (child process)** — declared with `external.enabled = true` and an
  absolute `command`. The `goraic` runtime spawns the process and passes
  `NATS_URL` so the child connects to the embedded (or configured) bus. Modeled
  exactly on gorai's external-service requirement (`REQ-EXTERNAL-SVC-*`).
- **External (remote, same bus)** — a service running in some *other* `goraic` or
  `gorai` process on the same NATS server. From the calling service's point of view
  it is just another subject prefix. Declared via a `remotes` block (optional) for
  discovery hints and by *not* declaring the service locally.

**REQ-ARCH-4 — One NATS, many machines.** A `goraic` instance may be configured to
connect to an existing NATS URL instead of starting an embedded server. When two or
more `goraic` (or `gorai`) processes share a NATS server, every service published on
that bus is callable from every process. This is how work spreads across machines and
LLMs (e.g., Claude on a laptop, Ollama on a workstation, GPT on a CI box).

**REQ-ARCH-5 — TUI front, file-only logs.** The TUI is the only writer to stdout. All
NATS traffic, LLM traces, service lifecycle, and errors go to the log file named in
the RDL `log` block. The single permitted stderr write is one fatal line at startup
if the log file cannot be opened.

**REQ-ARCH-6 — Open-standard agent files.** `goraic` is built on two open
standards and **MUST** comply with both:

- **Project agentic instructions MUST follow the AGENTS.md specification at
  https://agents.md/.** The repo-root `AGENTS.md` is the canonical place where a
  project tells `goraic` (and any other agents.md-compatible coding agent) how
  to build, test, lint, and behave inside the codebase. Nested `AGENTS.md`
  files in subdirectories override the root file for work under that subtree
  ("closest AGENTS.md to the edited file wins"), exactly as the spec requires.
- **Skills MUST follow the Agent Skills specification at
  https://agentskills.io/.** Every skill is a directory containing a `SKILL.md`
  with the spec-defined YAML frontmatter (`name`, `description`, and the
  optional `license`, `compatibility`, `metadata`, `allowed-tools` fields), and
  may include the optional `scripts/`, `references/`, and `assets/`
  subdirectories. Skills are loaded with the spec's three-stage **progressive
  disclosure** model.

Skills attach to **command services**, not to LLM adapters. Compatibility with
Claude Code conventions (`CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/skills/`) is supported as a
fallback discovery source only; it is **not** a substitute for spec compliance.
A skill that loads from `.claude/skills/` is still validated against
agentskills.io and rejected if non-conformant.

---

## 3. The Robot Definition Language (RDL)

`agent.json` is removed. Configuration uses an RDL file structurally identical to
gorai's RDL v2 (see `/external/gorai/pkg/config/config.go`, `RDL`, `ServiceConfig`,
`LogConfig`, `RemoteConfig`).

**REQ-RDL-1 — File and discovery.** Default config path is `./robot.json`. CLI flag
`--config <path>` overrides. If neither is present `goraic` exits with a clear error
naming the expected path.

**REQ-RDL-2 — Wire-compatible types (not Go-type identity).** goraic's config
loader defines its own Go types whose JSON shape **MUST match gorai's
`pkg/config` types** for the fields they share (`version`, `robot`, `log`,
`nats`, `services[].name|type|model|disabled|attributes|depends_on|external`,
`remotes`). goraic extends the format with three additions: a top-level
`defaults` block (required `command` field), and two new `services[].type`
values (`command`, `prompt-log`). A gorai parser reading a goraic RDL ignores
those additions; a goraic parser reading a gorai RDL works as long as
`defaults.command` is satisfiable. A test asserts JSON-shape compatibility
against a representative gorai-style RDL. The robot-section fields
(`robot.name`, `robot.description`) are required by the schema but carry no
operational meaning for a coding agent; the name appears in logs and status messages.
**Rationale:** binding directly to gorai's Go types couples goraic's release
cadence to gorai's. The wire is the contract.

**REQ-RDL-3 — Service types recognised by goraic.** The `services[].type` enum is
small and closed. Connection-to-an-LLM is a named service so that several
commands can share one endpoint+credential configuration and an operator has a
single place to rotate a key or repoint at a different Ollama host.

| `type`        | Purpose                                                       |
|---------------|---------------------------------------------------------------|
| `llm`         | A configured LLM endpoint (protocol, endpoint URL, API-key env var, default model). Local to the binary; referenced by name from command services. |
| `command`     | A slash-command role (`/plan`, `/code`, ...). The user-visible unit of work. Binds to an `llm` service by name. |
| `coordinator` | Multi-step orchestrator that composes command services        |
| `capability`  | Tool host (files, search, shell, repo, memory) — auto-registered; entries only present to disable or override |
| `prompt-log`  | Subscribes to all task traffic and writes per-session JSONL   |

All names are NATS-subject-safe (`^[a-z][a-z0-9_-]*$`). Duplicate names are rejected.

**Rationale for `llm` as a first-class service type:** v0.1 hid the LLM behind a
Go interface inside the agent; an interim cut of v0.2 inlined `provider`/`model`
into each command's `attributes`. The inline form duplicates endpoint and
credential config any time two commands point at the same provider, which is
the common case (a typical RDL has several Anthropic-backed commands). Making
`llm` a named service collapses that duplication, gives `/services` a real
target to enumerate, and provides one validation point for "is `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`
set?" at startup. Cross-machine load spreading still works (REQ-DIST-2) — the
*command* moves to the other machine, and the LLM service it depends on either
moves with it or is reached over the bus by name.

**REQ-RDL-4 — External services use gorai's shape.** When `services[].external` is set
the runtime spawns the named command and passes `NATS_URL` in the environment, per
`REQ-EXTERNAL-SVC-1..3` from gorai. Restart policy honours the gorai
`external.restart` field.

**REQ-RDL-5 — Remote NATS support.** A top-level `nats` block selects the bus:

```json
"nats": { "embedded": true }                         // default
"nats": { "embedded": false, "url": "nats://host:4222" }   // join shared bus
```

When `embedded` is false, the `goraic` instance does NOT start a server; it connects
as a client. All `services` declared in *this* RDL are then published onto that
shared bus, and services declared elsewhere (by other `goraic` or `gorai`
processes on the same bus) are discoverable by their NATS subject prefix.

**REQ-RDL-6 — Minimal RDL.**

```json
{
  "version": "2",
  "robot": { "name": "goraic-local" }
}
```

This starts the embedded NATS server, no other services. The CLI is then useless
(no command services), but startup must succeed and exit cleanly.

**REQ-RDL-7 — Representative RDL.** See §10 for a full example.

---

## 4. Services

### 4.1 Local vs external

**REQ-SVC-1.** A service is local when its `type` is implemented inside the goraic
binary and `external` is absent. The runtime constructs it via a registered factory
and runs it as a goroutine.

**REQ-SVC-2.** A service is external when `external.enabled = true`. The runtime
must NOT instantiate it in-process. It must spawn the named command and forward
`NATS_URL`. Cross-service calls are unchanged — they go over NATS.

**REQ-SVC-3.** A service is remote (no declaration needed in the local RDL) when it
exists on the shared NATS bus from another process. Calling code uses its name as a
subject prefix and the call works without local config, provided the caller's RDL
opts into the shared bus per REQ-RDL-5.

### 4.2 Heartbeats and discovery

**REQ-SVC-4.** Every service publishes `StatusMessage` heartbeats on `<name>.status`
with its type, model (if applicable), and the list of tools it exposes. This is the
basis for the `/services` user-facing command (§7.4) and for tool discovery by
command services.

### 4.3 LLM services (in goraic) and provider library (`../gorai-llm-service`)

An LLM is a **named service** declared in the RDL (`type: "llm"`). It is local
to the binary — its NATS-service wrapper compiles into `goraic` — but the
per-protocol translation lives in the sibling `gorai-llm-service` library.
goraic's `internal/llmsvc` package is a single generic wrapper that takes any
`gorai-llm-service` provider and exposes it on the bus as a service of type
`llm`.

**REQ-LLM-SVC-1 — RDL shape.** A service of `type: "llm"` declares the
following `attributes`:

| Attribute       | Required | Purpose                                                                                                                                              |
|-----------------|----------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `protocol`      | yes      | Wire format. Enum: `anthropic \| openai \| ollama \| gemini \| mock \| remote`. Selects the translator compiled into the binary from `gorai-llm-service`. Use `remote` when inference runs on a separate host via `goraci-llm-svc` (see REQ-LLM-SVC-6). |
| `endpoint`      | sometimes | Base URL. Optional (SaaS protocols default to their official URL; `ollama` defaults to `http://localhost:11434`). |
| `api_key_env`   | optional | Name of an env var holding the API key. **Never the key itself.** Defaults per protocol (`ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, `OPENAI_API_KEY`, `GEMINI_API_KEY`); ignored for `ollama`/`mock`. |
| `headers_env`   | optional | Map of `header-name → env-var-name` for proxies/gateways that need extra headers. Values resolved from env at startup. |
| `model`         | optional | Default model for callers that don't specify one. Command services may override.                                                                     |
| `temperature`   | optional | Default temperature for callers that don't specify one.                                                                                              |
| `max_tokens`    | optional | Default per-request max tokens.                                                                                                                      |
| `timeout_seconds` | optional | Per-request timeout.                                                                                                                               |
| `tls`           | optional | `{ ca_cert_file, insecure_skip_verify }` for private CAs. `insecure_skip_verify: true` produces a startup warning.                                   |

**REQ-LLM-SVC-2 — Credentials are env-only.** The RDL schema **forbids** any
field whose name matches `/api[_-]?key$/` from carrying a literal value; the
only accepted shape is `api_key_env: <ENV_VAR_NAME>`. A loader test asserts
the schema rejects a literal `api_key`. There is no inline-secret escape hatch.

**REQ-LLM-SVC-3 — Startup fail-fast.** Every declared LLM service resolves its
`api_key_env` (and any `headers_env` values) at startup. If the protocol
requires credentials and the named env var is unset or empty, `goraic` exits
with a non-zero status and a clear error naming the LLM service and the
missing env var. There is no lazy first-request failure.

**REQ-LLM-SVC-4 — Endpoint defaults are compiled-in per protocol.** The RDL
stays minimal for the common SaaS case (declaring `protocol` + `model` is
enough). Operators only set `endpoint` to override a default or to point at a
private host.

**REQ-LLM-SVC-5 — Local NATS exposure.** The runtime publishes each `llm`
service on its own subject prefix derived from `name` (per existing gorai-NATS
conventions). Command services dispatch completion requests to that subject.
Heartbeats are emitted under `<name>.status` per REQ-SVC-4. Per REQ-DIST-2,
an LLM service running on another node in the same NATS bus is reachable from
remote commands without any extra config in the caller's RDL beyond a
reference name.

**REQ-LLM-SVC-6 — Remote protocol is a no-op local service.** When
`attributes.protocol = "remote"`, `llmsvc.New` returns a `remoteService`
that satisfies `bus.Service` but has an empty `Start()` — it subscribes to
nothing and emits no heartbeats. The actual subscriber is `goraic-llm-svc`
running on the remote host. This prevents the dual-subscriber fan-out problem
that would occur if goraic's in-process llmsvc and the remote daemon both
subscribed to `<name>.request` on a plain (non-queue) subject.

The remote daemon MUST use a NATS queue group (`QueueSubscribe`) so that
multiple daemon instances on the same subject load-balance rather than fan-out.

Credential resolution (`REQ-LLM-SVC-3`) is skipped entirely for
`protocol: "remote"` — no provider is constructed, no env vars are read.

**REQ-LLM-1 — gorai-llm-service is a library, not a binary.**
`github.com/emergingrobotics/gorai-llm-service`, sibling checkout at
`../gorai-llm-service`. The `goraic` `go.mod` adds
`replace github.com/emergingrobotics/gorai-llm-service => ../gorai-llm-service`.
There is no `cmd/` in this module; no NATS code; no service plumbing. The
single responsibility of the module is to provide per-protocol translators
that goraic's LLM-service wrapper consumes.

**REQ-LLM-2 — Common interface, per-provider package.** Layout:

```
gorai-llm-service/
  pkg/llm/                 Provider interface + canonical Request/Response types
  pkg/types/               Canonical TaskRequest/TaskResult re-exported for callers
  providers/anthropic/     Anthropic Messages API translation
  providers/openai/        OpenAI / chat-completions translation
  providers/ollama/        Ollama (OpenAI-compat) + qwen degradation defender
    providers/mock/          scripted adapter for deterministic E2E tests
```

Each provider exports a constructor `New(cfg llm.Config) (llm.Provider, error)`
that returns an object implementing `Complete(ctx context.Context, req
llm.Request) (llm.Response, error)`.

**REQ-LLM-3 — Canonical Response at the library boundary.** Callers (the
