goraic LLM-service wrapper) see only the canonical types from `pkg/llm`.
Provider-specific quirks (Anthropic content blocks, Ollama tool-call-as-text
degradation, OpenAI function-vs-tool naming) are handled inside the provider
package.

**REQ-LLM-4 — goraic stops embedding provider SDKs directly.** After the
extraction, `goraic/internal/llm/{anthropic,ollama,mock}` is deleted. The
replacement is a single `goraic/internal/llmsvc` package: a thin
NATS-service wrapper that takes any `llm.Provider` and exposes it as an LLM
service on the bus. Provider-specific dependencies (`anthropic-sdk-go`,
`go-openai`) move into their respective provider packages inside
`gorai-llm-service`. After the move, `go.mod` in `goraic` lists no
provider SDK directly.

**REQ-LLM-5 — Shared providers (in-process cache).** When two `llm` services
in the same RDL declare the same `(protocol, endpoint, api_key_env)` tuple,
the runtime SHOULD share a single underlying provider client to avoid
duplicate connection setup and to coalesce rate-limit budgeting. Two `llm`
services that differ only in `model` are a legitimate "two LLMs, one
endpoint" pattern and share an underlying client.

### 4.4 Capability services (auto-registered)

The five capability services — `files`, `search`, `shell`, `repo`, `memory` — are
compiled into the goraic binary and **auto-register at startup**. They are NOT
listed in a typical `robot.json`. The RDL only carries a `type: "capability"`
entry when the operator wants to disable one or override its attributes.

**REQ-CAP-1 — Closed set.** The known capability set is fixed:
`{files, search, shell, repo, memory}`. The validator rejects a
`type: "capability"` entry whose name is not in this set (catches typos like
`shel` that would otherwise silently no-op).

**REQ-CAP-2 — Disable.** An RDL entry with `disabled: true` causes the runtime
to skip the capability's factory; the service does not start and does not
publish heartbeats. `--safe` continues to work as a CLI alias for
`shell.disabled=true`.

**REQ-CAP-3 — Per-capability overrides via `attributes`.** A user MAY add an
entry with `attributes: {...}` to override defaults (e.g.,
`{"name":"shell","type":"capability","attributes":{"allowlist":["ls","cat"]}}`).
The runtime passes the attributes to the factory; factories that don't yet
honor a given attribute log a warning and use the default. CLI flags
(`--safe`, `--allow-outside-working-dir`) remain the path for boot-time
overrides; the RDL is the path for per-environment policy.

**REQ-CAP-4 — No auto-discovery for LLMs (explicit policy).** Capability
services auto-register because they have no per-user configuration — every
goraic ships with the same `files` service. LLMs are the opposite: choosing
which LLM to use is a deliberate operator decision involving API keys, costs,
model quality, and data egress. goraic **does NOT** auto-detect an Ollama at
`localhost:11434`, **does NOT** auto-pick a provider when `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`
is set, etc. Every LLM is declared explicitly in the RDL as a `type: "llm"`
service (REQ-LLM-SVC-1).

### 4.5 Prompt-log service (from enhancements.md)

**REQ-SVC-5 — Prompt logging.** A service of type `prompt-log` subscribes to every
`*.task` and `*.result` subject and writes each as a JSON line to
`./.goraic/sessions/<YYYY-MM-DD>.jsonl` in the working directory. The file's schema
is the gorai-NATS envelope; replay is `nats pub` of the captured lines. The
`.goraic/` folder is auto-created and added to `.gitignore` on first write.

---

## 5. Slash-command services (the central new concept)

This is the load-bearing change vs v0.1. **The user does not talk to "the agent"; the
user talks to a chosen command service by typing its name as a slash command.** The
RDL decides which model is behind each command.

### 5.1 Mapping

**REQ-CMD-1 — A command service is a named service of `type: "command"`.** Its `name`
field is exactly the slash-command token the user will type (without the leading
slash). So `name: "plan"` is invoked as `/plan ...`, `name: "code-review"` as
`/code-review ...`, and so on.

**REQ-CMD-2 — A command service references a declared LLM service.** The
command's `attributes` name an existing `llm` service rather than inlining
provider/endpoint/credential config:

- `llm` (required) — the `name` of a service of `type: "llm"` declared elsewhere
  in this RDL or reachable on the bus per REQ-DIST-2.

Optional attributes that override the LLM service's defaults for invocations
made by this command only:

- `model` — overrides `attributes.model` on the LLM service (e.g., the LLM
  service defaults to `claude-sonnet-4-6`, but `/explain` uses
  `claude-haiku-4-5`).
- `temperature` — overrides the LLM service's `temperature`.
- `max_tokens` — overrides the LLM service's `max_tokens`.

The inline `provider`/`api_key_env`/`base_url` attributes from earlier drafts
are **not accepted**. A command service that declares any of those is a fatal
RDL validation error with a migration message pointing at this section.
Mapping one slash command to multiple models is still out of scope for v0.2 —
declare a second command instead (e.g., `code-cloud` and `code-local`).

**REQ-CMD-3 — Optional skills and permissions.** A command service may declare:

- `attributes.skills`: a list of skill filenames or skill IDs to load into the
  system prompt for this command.
- `attributes.permissions`: one of `readonly | standard | broad` — gates the tools
  exposed to this command via the capability services.
- `attributes.system_prompt`: an optional inline system prompt override; if absent
  a default is composed from the command name and loaded skills.

**REQ-CMD-4 — Default command set.** The shipped example RDL declares, at minimum,
these command services: `/plan`, `/design`, `/code`, `/code-review`, `/test`,
`/write`, `/explain`. None of these names are hardcoded in goraic; they exist
only because they appear in the RDL. The user is free to add `/refactor`,
`/security-audit`, `/translate`, etc., by adding entries.

### 5.2 Invocation

**REQ-CMD-5 — Chat input parsing.** The TUI input parser:

1. Trims leading whitespace.
2. If the line begins with `/`, the token up to the next whitespace is the command
   name; the rest is the **prompt**.
3. If the command name resolves to a built-in (`/exit`, `/quit`, `/clear`,
   `/help`, `/services`, `/cancel`, see §7), it runs the built-in.
4. Otherwise the parser looks up the command service by name. If found, it
   dispatches a `TaskRequest` to that command service on `<name>.task`. If not
   found, it prints an error listing the available commands.

**REQ-CMD-6 — Bare-prompt default (mandatory).** The RDL **must** set
`defaults.command` to the name of one of the declared `command` services. If a
chat input line does not begin with `/`, it is dispatched to that command
service. A missing `defaults.command`, an unknown name, or a name whose target
is not of `type: "command"` is a **fatal RDL validation error** at startup —
`goraic` refuses to launch with a clear message naming the offending field. This
makes the implicit-target behaviour explicit (no surprise "which model just
answered me?") and forces every RDL to declare the operator's intent for bare
prompts. There is no implicit "the agent."

**REQ-CMD-7 — The command service is the agent.** A command service IS a ReAct
agent service. It receives `TaskRequest`, runs a reasoning loop against its bound
LLM, discovers tools from `*.status` heartbeats, dispatches tool calls to
capability services, and returns a `TaskResult`. The existing `internal/agent`
package becomes the implementation of command services (renamed accordingly).

### 5.3 Concurrency and chaining

**REQ-CMD-8 — Each invocation runs in its own goroutine.** Submitting a slash
command does NOT block the prompt. The TUI returns to the input line immediately;
progress and final output for the invocation stream into the scrollback as they
arrive.

**REQ-CMD-9 — Multiple commands in flight.** The user may submit several slash
commands back to back. The status line shows one entry per active task with its
command name, elapsed time, and current action. The scrollback preserves output
ordering by completion; partial / interleaved output is allowed but each task's
lines are prefixed with the task id (short suffix) so the user can disambiguate.
There is no global queue; tasks run as soon as they are dispatched.

**REQ-CMD-10 — Cancellation.** `/cancel <task-id>` cancels one task. `/cancel all`
cancels every in-flight task. Ctrl-C while typing cancels the most recent task
and confirms exit only on a second Ctrl-C at an empty prompt (existing behaviour
preserved for the single-task case).

**REQ-CMD-11 — Chaining (pipe syntax).** The chat parser recognises a single-line
pipeline:

```
/plan write PLAN.md from DESIGN.md | /code-review review PLAN.md | /code implement phase 1 of PLAN.md
```

Each `|` separates one slash-command invocation. Pipelines run sequentially: the
next stage starts when the previous stage's `TaskResult.Status == complete`. The
prior stage's `FinalAnswer` is prepended to the next stage's prompt as
`PriorContext` (existing protocol field). A failing stage stops the pipeline and
prints the failure. The user prompt returns immediately after dispatch — the
pipeline runs in its own goroutine like a single command.

**REQ-CMD-12 — Parallel branches (optional, deferred).** A future extension is
`&&` for sequential AND, `&` for parallel branches. **Out of scope for v0.2 unless
trivially derivable from REQ-CMD-11.** The grammar in v0.2 is restricted to the
pipe.

**REQ-CMD-13 — Pipelines span machines.** Because each stage is a NATS dispatch by
name, a pipeline transparently spans machines as soon as the corresponding command
services are running on remote `goraic` instances connected to the same NATS bus.

### 5.4 Worked example

```
/plan write a file PLAN.md that lays out implementation phases for the project in DESIGN.md
```

1. Parser splits `plan` and the prompt.
2. Looks up command service `plan` in the RDL. Finds it bound (via
   `attributes.llm`) to the LLM service `anthropic-sonnet` (also declared in
   the RDL, with `attributes.protocol: "anthropic"` and
   `attributes.model: "claude-sonnet-4-6"`).
3. Dispatches `TaskRequest{Prompt: "write a file...", ...}` to `plan.task`.
4. The `plan` command service receives it, builds messages (system prompt +
   skills + user prompt), sends a completion request to `anthropic-sonnet.request`,
   handles tool calls against `files.tools.write_file`, etc., and publishes the
   final `TaskResult` on `plan.result`.
5. The TUI surfaces progress and the final answer into the scrollback as it
   arrives.
6. The user can type `/code-review review PLAN.md` immediately — it dispatches in
   parallel; results interleave; status line shows two active tasks.

---

## 6. Distributed execution

**REQ-DIST-1 — A `goraic` instance must be runnable in one of three NATS modes.**

| Mode               | RDL `nats` block                                               | Use                                                  |
|--------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| Embedded (default) | `{ "embedded": true }` or omitted                              | Solo laptop use                                      |
| Shared external    | `{ "embedded": false, "url": "nats://host:4222" }`             | Multiple `goraic`/`gorai` processes on a shared bus  |
| Embedded + listen  | `{ "embedded": true, "listen": ":4222" }`                      | One `goraic` acts as the bus; others connect to it   |

**REQ-DIST-2 — Mesh authority is the bus.** Whether a command or LLM service is
local, external-child-process, or running on another machine is invisible to
the caller. The only thing the caller does is publish to `<name>.task` (for
commands) or `<llm>.request` (for LLMs). This makes heterogeneous fleets — a
laptop with `/plan` bound to a Claude LLM service on the laptop, a workstation
with a beefy-Ollama LLM service that several commands across the bus reference,
a CI box with `/code-review` bound to a cheaper cloud LLM — work without any
code change on the caller side. Operator runs the right services on the right
machines; each command names the LLM service it depends on.

**REQ-DIST-3 — LLM services are addressable by name across the bus.** A
command's `attributes.llm` value may resolve to a service declared in this
RDL or to one declared on another goraic instance reachable via the shared
NATS bus (REQ-RDL-5). The RDL validator MAY accept a reference to a name not
declared locally provided either (a) `nats.embedded` is false, or (b) the
`remotes` block names the LLM service explicitly; otherwise an unknown
`attributes.llm` is a fatal RDL validation error. To dedicate a machine to
hosting one or more LLM services, run a goraic instance there whose RDL
declares only `type: "llm"` services.

**REQ-DIST-4 — No assumption of trust between processes.** When `goraic` connects
to an external NATS, credentials for that bus come from a `nats.credentials` field
or environment variable; no code path emits or logs credentials.

---

## 7. User interface

### 7.1 TUI shape (unchanged from v0.1)

**REQ-UI-1.** Scrollback-streaming output. While a turn is in flight, an inline
status line (spinner + current action + in/out token counts + elapsed) is rendered
directly below the user prompt and updates in place via \r\x1b[K (no scroll
region, no alternate screen). The status line is cleared when the turn completes
and replaced by the final answer; the next goraic> prompt then appears below.
Real terminal copy-paste must continue to work in the transcript.

**REQ-UI-2.** All internal output goes to the log file; nothing internal appears on
the TUI.

### 7.2 Prompt and history (from enhancements.md)

**REQ-UI-3.** The input line is read with `chzyer/readline`. Up/Down arrows scroll
through prompt history. History is loaded from `./.goraic/history` and appended
on every accepted input. History entries are editable in place before resubmit.

### 7.3 Multi-task display

**REQ-UI-4.** While ≥1 task is in flight the status line cycles through the active
tasks (or shows a compact stack if more than one) with command name, short task
id, current action, and elapsed seconds. The transcript above is prefixed per task
when more than one task is in flight to avoid interleaving confusion.

### 7.4 Built-in commands

`/exit`, `/quit` — leave the session (existing).
`/clear` — reset the running conversation history (the `PriorContext` thread of
the chat session). The next turn dispatches with an empty prior, so the
status-line token bracket and context-window percentage reset to 0 on the
next slash-command invocation. Rejected if a task is currently in flight.
`/help` — list available commands (built-ins + every `command` service from the
RDL, with their bound LLM).
`/services` — list every service currently heartbeating on the bus, grouped by
type (llm / command / capability / coordinator / prompt-log), with the model for
LLMs and the latest seen timestamp. Implementation polls the `*.status` subjects
already required by REQ-SVC-4. This satisfies the enhancements.md request.
`/cancel <id|all>` — cancel as in REQ-CMD-10.
`/history [N]` — print the last N prompts from `./.goraic/history`.

Slash names listed above are reserved: a `command` service must not use any of
them. The RDL validator rejects reserved-name collisions.

---

## 8. Agent files, skills, permissions, security

`goraic` is built on two open standards. Section 8.1 covers project agentic
instructions (the **AGENTS.md** standard at https://agents.md/). Section 8.2
covers skills (the **Agent Skills** standard at https://agentskills.io/).
Sections 8.3–8.4 cover the permission and security envelope that wraps both.

### 8.1 Project agentic instructions — AGENTS.md (agents.md)

**REQ-AGENTS-1 — AGENTS.md is the canonical project-instructions file.**
`goraic` MUST load `AGENTS.md` per the open specification at
https://agents.md/. The file is plain Markdown at the repository root; it
contains whatever project-overview, build, test, code-style, security, and
"do-not-touch" guidance the project author considers necessary. There are no
required headings or fields — `goraic` MUST NOT impose any schema beyond what
agents.md itself defines.

**REQ-AGENTS-2 — Nearest-AGENTS.md-wins.** When a command service is about to
act on a file at path `P`, the loader walks from `dir(P)` up to the repo root
collecting every `AGENTS.md` encountered. The **nearest** (deepest) file
overrides shallower ones for any guidance that conflicts. This is the
agents.md precedence rule and is mandatory; it is what makes monorepos work.

**REQ-AGENTS-3 — Repo-root AGENTS.md is the session baseline.** When the
session starts, or for any task that does not target a specific file (`/plan`,
`/design`, free-form chat), the repo-root `AGENTS.md` from the current working
directory tree is treated as the baseline. `goraic` MUST inject the relevant
`AGENTS.md` content into the command service's system prompt at task dispatch
time.

**REQ-AGENTS-4 — User prompt overrides files.** Per the agents.md spec, an
explicit instruction in the user's prompt overrides anything written in
`AGENTS.md`. The system prompt MUST be structured so the user prompt is the
last and most authoritative slot.

**REQ-AGENTS-5 — CLAUDE.md compatibility fallback.** If no `AGENTS.md` is
present at a given level of the tree but a `CLAUDE.md` is, `goraic` MAY load
`CLAUDE.md` as a fallback. `AGENTS.md`, if present, always wins. A nearby
`CLAUDE.md` never overrides a shallower `AGENTS.md`. Compatibility is a
convenience for repositories that have not yet adopted agents.md; it is not
the goraic-native path.

**REQ-AGENTS-6 — Reload on change.** Between task invocations, the loader
re-stats the `AGENTS.md` files it last loaded and reloads any whose mtime
changed. Edits to `AGENTS.md` MUST take effect on the next task without
restarting `goraic`.

### 8.2 Skills — agentskills.io

**REQ-SKILL-1 — Skills MUST conform to the Agent Skills specification.**
Every skill `goraic` loads MUST be a directory containing a `SKILL.md` file in
the format defined at https://agentskills.io/specification. The YAML
frontmatter MUST include the required fields:

- `name` — 1–64 chars, lowercase `[a-z0-9-]`, no leading/trailing or
  consecutive hyphens; MUST equal the parent directory name.
- `description` — 1–1024 chars, non-empty.

The optional fields `license`, `compatibility`, `metadata`, and `allowed-tools`
MUST be accepted and surfaced to command services; unknown fields MUST NOT
cause load failures (forward-compatibility).

**REQ-SKILL-2 — Optional resource subdirectories.** A skill folder MAY contain
`scripts/`, `references/`, and `assets/` subdirectories. `goraic` MUST NOT
auto-execute anything in `scripts/`; execution happens only when the command
service calls a capability service that invokes the script. `references/` and
`assets/` are static files the LLM may request through the file-read
capability.

**REQ-SKILL-3 — Progressive disclosure (mandatory three stages).** Skill
loading MUST follow the agentskills.io progressive-disclosure model:

1. **Discovery.** At session start, the loader scans every skill source
   (REQ-SKILL-4) and indexes `name` + `description` only. Only these two
   fields appear in the skill catalog presented to the LLM.
2. **Activation.** When a skill is named in a command service's
   `attributes.skills`, listed on a `/skill` invocation, or selected by the
   LLM from the catalog during a task, the full `SKILL.md` body is loaded
   into the command service's system prompt for that task.
3. **Execution.** Files under `scripts/`, `references/`, or `assets/` load
   only when the LLM explicitly references them via the file-read or
   shell-exec capability service.

**REQ-SKILL-4 — Discovery sources and layering.** The loader searches the
following sources, lowest priority first; later sources override earlier ones
when a `name` collides:

1. Built-in skills compiled into the `goraic` binary (`internal/skills/builtin`).
2. User-global skills: `~/.config/goraic/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (and
   `~/.goraic/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` for backward compatibility).
3. Project-local skills: `./.goraic/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`.
4. Claude-Code compatibility sources (fallback only):
   `~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` and `./.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`.
5. Paths named in a command service's `attributes.skill_paths`.

Claude sources are read at low priority and every loaded skill — regardless of
source — MUST pass agentskills.io validation. A non-conformant file is
rejected with a logged error and excluded from the catalog; it MUST NOT make
the session fail.

**REQ-SKILL-5 — Skills attach to command services, not LLMs.** A command
service's `attributes.skills` lists skill `name` values to pre-activate for
every task that command handles. A skill referenced in `attributes.skills`
that does not exist in any discovery source is a **fatal RDL validation
error** at startup, with a message naming the missing skill.

**REQ-SKILL-6 — `allowed-tools` is advisory until enforced.** The
spec-defined `allowed-tools` field is parsed and stored on the loaded skill.
For v0.2 it is exposed to command services as metadata only; v0.3 may use it
to further restrict the capability services a command may call when this
skill is active. v0.2 MUST NOT silently grant tools beyond what the command
service's `permissions` mode already allows.

**REQ-SKILL-7 — Validation CLI.** `goraic skills list` MUST print the active
skill catalog (name, description, source, source path). `goraic skills
validate [path]` MUST run agentskills.io conformance checks on a skill
directory and exit non-zero on any violation. Reuse of the upstream
`skills-ref validate` rules is acceptable.

### 8.3 Permissions

**REQ-PERM-1.** Permission modes (`readonly | standard | broad`) gate the
tools the command service is willing to invoke. Gating happens at the
**command service**, not at the capability service, because permissions are
per-role. The `allowed-tools` frontmatter on an active skill (REQ-SKILL-6) MAY
narrow this set further but MUST NOT widen it.

### 8.4 Security

**REQ-SEC-1.** No credentials in logs, ever. API keys come from the environment;
their names may be logged but their values must not.

**REQ-SEC-2.** External services are processes, not containers (REQ-EXTERNAL-SVC-1
from gorai). Absolute paths only.

**REQ-SEC-3.** No `net/http` import in the `goraic` binary outside the LLM provider
packages. (Existing rule, preserved by the security test.)

**REQ-SEC-4.** Skill loading MUST audit `SKILL.md` files for hidden Unicode
threats (bidirectional override characters, zero-width characters,
homoglyph-prone confusables) before activation. A skill whose `SKILL.md`
contains a critical finding is **blocked** from activation and logged; the
session continues with that skill excluded. `goraic audit skills` MUST
support a `--strip` flag that rewrites offending files to remove dangerous
characters in place. (This is the pi-go audit pattern; we adopt it
verbatim because the agentskills.io spec does not address supply-chain risk.)

---

## 9. Migration from v0.1

**REQ-MIG-1.** Loading `agent.json` is no longer supported. The CLI prints a clear
migration message if it finds `./agent.json` and no `./robot.json` is present,
including a sample minimal `robot.json` the user can copy.

**REQ-MIG-2.** The `behavior` service type from v0.1 is **renamed to `command`**.
The semantics are richer (one LLM binding per command, slash-command exposure) but
existing RDL entries can be ported by changing `type: "behavior"` to
`type: "command"` and ensuring the `name` is the desired slash-command token.

**REQ-MIG-3.** The hardcoded `planner / coder / reviewer` coordinator pipeline is
not deleted but is reframed as **one possible composition of command services**
(`/plan`, `/code`, `/code-review`). The `coordinator` service type remains for
when a single user prompt should fan out across a fixed sequence; otherwise the
user composes pipelines with the pipe syntax (REQ-CMD-11).

**REQ-MIG-4.** `internal/llm/*` packages are deleted in favour of importing
`gorai-llm-service`. The `internal/agent` package is renamed conceptually to
"command service" (file-level rename is a code-side concern; the public surface is
the new RDL `type: "command"`).

---

## 10. Worked RDL example

```json
{
  "version": "2",
  "robot": {
    "name": "goraic-dev",
    "description": "Local goraic with cloud + local LLMs and remote helpers"
  },
  "log": {
    "file": "~/.local/share/gorai/goraic.log",
    "level": "info",
    "max_size_mb": 64,
    "max_backups": 3
  },
  "nats": { "embedded": true, "listen": "127.0.0.1:4222" },
  "defaults": { "command": "code" },

  "services": [
    { "name": "anthropic-sonnet", "type": "llm",
      "attributes": {
        "protocol": "anthropic",
        "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6"
      } },

    { "name": "openai-gpt5", "type": "llm",
      "attributes": {
        "protocol": "openai",
        "model": "gpt-5"
      } },

    { "name": "ollama-coder", "type": "llm",
      "attributes": {
        "protocol": "ollama",
        "endpoint": "http://localhost:11434",
        "model": "qwen3-coder:30b",
        "temperature": 0.2
      } },

    { "name": "plan",        "type": "command",
      "attributes": {
        "llm": "anthropic-sonnet",
        "permissions": "read-only",
        "skills": ["writing-plans", "systematic-debugging"]
      } },

    { "name": "design",      "type": "command",
      "attributes": {
        "llm": "anthropic-sonnet",
        "permissions": "read-only",
        "skills": ["architecture-first"]
      } },

    { "name": "code",        "type": "command",
      "attributes": {
        "llm": "ollama-coder",
        "permissions": "standard",
        "skills": ["test-driven-development", "edge-case-discovery"]
      } },

    { "name": "code-review", "type": "command",
      "attributes": {
        "llm": "openai-gpt5",
        "permissions": "read-only",
        "skills": ["code-review"]
      } },

    { "name": "test",        "type": "command",
      "attributes": {
        "llm": "ollama-coder",
        "permissions": "standard",
        "skills": ["test-as-guardrails"]
      } },

    { "name": "write",       "type": "command",
      "attributes": {
        "llm": "anthropic-sonnet",
        "permissions": "read-only",
        "skills": ["documentation"]
      } },

    { "name": "explain",     "type": "command",
      "attributes": {
        "llm": "ollama-coder",
        "permissions": "read-only"
      } },

    { "name": "prompt-log",  "type": "prompt-log",
      "attributes": { "dir": ".goraic/sessions" } }
  ],

  "remotes": [
    { "name": "buildbox",
      "address": "nats://buildbox.lan:4222",
      "services": ["ollama-large", "code-large"] }
  ]
}
```

Note how three LLM services (`anthropic-sonnet`, `openai-gpt5`,
`ollama-coder`) back seven commands — exactly the kind of fan-in the v0.2
inline form duplicated. A second machine `buildbox.lan` runs its own goraic
instance against the same NATS URL with additional LLM services (e.g.
`ollama-large` backed by a beefy GPU) and additional command services
(e.g. `code-large` bound to `ollama-large`). The laptop's chat dispatches
`/code-large` over NATS; the buildbox handles it transparently. Work spreads
across the bus either by moving command services to another node, or by
hosting LLM services on a dedicated node and pointing remote commands at
them by name.

---

## 11. The `gorai-llm-service` sibling repo

**REQ-LLM-REPO-1 — Repository.** `github.com/emergingrobotics/gorai-llm-service`,
sibling checkout at `../gorai-llm-service`.

**REQ-LLM-REPO-2 — Authored layout (library only, no binary).**

```
../gorai-llm-service/
  go.mod
  Makefile                       targets: build, test, clean, run-tests
  README.md
  REQUIREMENTS.md                derived from this section
  pkg/
    llm/
      llm.go                     Provider interface; Config, Request, Response,
                                 Usage, ToolCall canonical types
      degradation.go             tool-call-as-text detector + counters
    types/
      types.go                   shared exports for callers that don't want pkg/llm
  providers/
    anthropic/
      provider.go                New(cfg) llm.Provider; Complete(ctx, req)
      translate.go               messages <-> protocol
      provider_test.go
    openai/
      provider.go
      translate.go
      provider_test.go
    ollama/
      provider.go                + OpenAI-compatible client
      degraded.go                model floor + qwen quirks
      provider_test.go
      provider.go
      provider_test.go
    mock/
      provider.go                scripted; deterministic E2E
```

No NATS code in this module. No standalone binary. No `cmd/`. The module is
purely a provider-protocol library. The NATS-service wrapper for the RDL
`type: "llm"` lives in goraic (`internal/llmsvc`) per REQ-LLM-4.

**REQ-LLM-REPO-3 — Single source of truth.** Every LLM provider-protocol
translation lives here. The `goraic` binary contains no provider SDK
imports and no per-protocol translation code after the extraction; it
constructs an `llm.Provider` from an LLM service's attributes via the
appropriate provider package and calls `Complete` in-process from the
NATS-service wrapper.

**REQ-LLM-REPO-4 — Protocol type ownership (JSON-Schema-as-contract).** The
canonical wire format lives in the JSON Schemas under
`goraic/docs/protocol/*.schema.json`. Both repos define their own Go
structs that marshal to the schemas; **the wire is the contract, not the Go
type**. Each repo MUST run a schema-validation test that loads the shared
schemas (gorai-llm-service vendors them via build-time copy or by referencing
the goraic checkout in CI) and round-trips representative instances.
This keeps the two modules independent — Go's `internal/` rule does not allow
`gorai-llm-service` to import `goraic/internal/protocol` even with a
`replace` directive — and prevents type drift through the schema gate.

---

## 12. Acceptance criteria

A v0.2 build of `goraic` is accepted when, against a representative RDL like §10:

1. `goraic chat` (with no `--config`) errors clearly because `./robot.json` is
   missing.
1a. An RDL with no `defaults.command`, or with `defaults.command` pointing at a
    non-existent or non-`command` service, fails validation at startup with a
    clear message naming the field.
2. With the RDL above present, `goraic chat` starts; the TUI shows the prompt.
3. `/help` lists `plan, design, code, code-review, test, write, explain` plus
   built-ins.
4. `/services` lists every LLM, capability, and command service, with `last seen`
   timestamps that update in real time.
5. `/plan write PLAN.md from DESIGN.md` dispatches; the prompt returns
   immediately; the status line shows the running plan task; the file
   `PLAN.md` is written by the time the task completes.
6. Submitting `/code-review review PLAN.md` while `/plan` is still running runs
   both tasks concurrently; both completions appear in the scrollback.
7. The pipeline `/plan ... | /code ...` runs the two stages sequentially with
   `PriorContext` threaded.
8. Pointing `nats` at an external URL where another goraic instance runs an
   LLM service (e.g., `ollama-large`), then declaring a command bound to that
   LLM name, makes the command transparently use the remote LLM. The local
   machine never imports that LLM's SDK.
8a. A command service that declares the legacy inline `provider`/`api_key_env`/
    `base_url` attributes fails RDL validation with a clear message pointing
    at REQ-CMD-2 and naming the offending attribute. A command's
    `attributes.llm` that names an undeclared LLM service (and is not present
    via a `remotes` block) also fails validation with a clear message.
8b. An `llm` service whose `protocol` requires credentials and whose declared
    `api_key_env` is unset at startup causes `goraic` to exit non-zero with
    a message naming the service and the env var.
9. `goraic/internal/llm/{anthropic,ollama,mock}` no longer exists; replaced by
   `goraic/internal/llmsvc` (a single generic wrapper). The security test for
   "no `net/http` outside `/llm`" is replaced by a test that asserts no
   provider-SDK imports are present in the `goraic` binary (all such imports
   live in `gorai-llm-service`).
10. The build is one `make build` away; tests pass via `make test`; race detector
    is on for packages with concurrency; no test is skipped or stubbed.

---

## 13. Open questions (call out, do not silently decide)

- **Pipeline error semantics:** does a `degraded` stage continue the pipeline or
  stop it? Default proposal: stop with a visible reason; user can override with
  `/option pipeline-on-degraded continue`.
- **Tool discovery across remotes:** does a local command service automatically
  see tools advertised by a remote capability service on the same bus? Default
  proposal: yes, since heartbeats are bus-wide, but mark behind a flag for v0.2
  to keep blast radius small.
- **Pipeline grammar beyond `|`:** out of scope for v0.2 unless a real workflow
  forces it. Revisit in v0.3 with concrete use cases.
- **Persistent multi-session memory:** the `memory` capability service exists,
  but persistence across `goraic` runs is not specified here. Treat as a v0.3
  topic.

These are tagged here so DESIGN.md and PLAN.md can either resolve them or carry
them forward as TODOs — they must not be silently decided by the implementor.

---

## 14. What this means for the rest of the docs

- **DESIGN.md** — update the "Configuration" section to the RDL above, drop the
  `agent.json` reference, add command services, add the `gorai-llm-service`
  dependency, document the concurrency model for command dispatch, and add a
  dedicated section describing how AGENTS.md (per https://agents.md/) and the
  Agent Skills standard (per https://agentskills.io/) are loaded, layered, and
  injected into command service system prompts.
- **PLAN.md** — most phases survive, but Phases 5–7 (LLM adapters) and Phase 9
  (Agent Service) are restructured around `gorai-llm-service` and command
  services. Phase 12 (Coordinator) becomes optional. A new phase covers the
  RDL migration (drop `agent.json`, adopt `pkg/config`) and a new phase covers
  the chat parser + concurrency model.
- **agent.json** — delete; replace with `robot.json` at the repo root as the
  shipped example RDL.
- **README.md** — currently a stub about pi computation; rewrite to describe
  goraic, the RDL, and the slash-command workflow once the implementation lands.

---

*Source of truth for goraic. Update this file before changing the design; update
the design before changing the implementation.*

content.content:
1	# Gorai: A Vision for the Agent Mesh
2	
3	**From coding agents to robot fleets — one substrate, one behavior language, one mesh**
4	
5	---
6	
7	## The Thesis
8	
9	Modern AI agents — whether they write code, drive robots, monitor sensors, plan missions, or coordinate fleets — share an architectural core: a reasoning loop that consumes language-model completions, dispatches tools, collaborates with other agents, and observes outcomes. They differ in *what* they reach for (a file vs. a motor; a regex vs. a LiDAR scan) and *where* they live (a developer's workstation vs. a robot deck), but they do not differ in their *shape*.
10	
11	Yet every domain today reinvents the infrastructure. The coding-agent world builds its own skill systems, its own prompt formats, its own tool plugin protocols. The robotics world builds its own task definition languages, its own behavior trees, its own middleware. The automation world builds its own workflow engines. None of these speak to each other. A "test-driven development" skill written for Claude Code cannot drive the verification phase of a robot's autonomous test suite, even though the underlying behavior — *write the check before you make the change, then prove the check fires* — is identical.
12	
13	Gorai's bet is that one substrate serves all of them. The same NATS-based mesh that connects motors and cameras connects LLM adapters and reasoning agents. The same Claude-compatible skill format that tells a coding agent "write tests first" tells a robot agent "perform a safety stop before crossing thresholds." The same agent-to-agent protocol that lets a planner agent dispatch implementation phases to a coder agent lets a perception agent dispatch goal updates to a navigation agent. The same MCP-equivalent service pattern that exposes filesystem operations to a coding agent exposes joint kinematics to a manipulation agent.
14	
15	This is not a generalization. It is a recognition: the abstractions are already isomorphic. We just have to stop pretending they aren't.
16	
17	---
18	
19	## The Mesh as Universal Fabric
20	
21	Gorai runs on NATS. This is not an implementation detail — it is the design premise.
22	
23	NATS provides the four things any agent system requires:
24	
25	- **Async task lifecycle.** Dispatch on `command`, stream tokens on `data`, confirm completion on `state`, monitor health on `status`. Long-running agent work maps directly onto this without polling or callbacks.
26	- **Discovery.** Gorai's mesh service registry is A2A-style capability discovery. An orchestrator can enumerate available agents and services, their capabilities, their preconditions, and their success rates — all from the mesh, at runtime.
27	- **Routing.** NATS subjects are the routing fabric. Fan-out, load balancing, and subject-based capability routing come for free from the bus, not from bespoke HTTP infrastructure.
28	- **Scale.** The same NATS cluster that connects three agents on a laptop connects a hundred agents across a robot fleet and a data center. The subject namespace scales; the programming model does not change.
29	
30	When the substrate is already NATS, the mesh IS the gateway. Capability discovery, tool federation, A2A coordination, and multi-agent routing are properties of the existing bus, not new components to build.
31	
32	---
33	
34	## Everything Is a Service
35	
36	The gorai design choice that pays off here: there is no separate "agent infrastructure," "tool infrastructure," "sensor infrastructure." Every participant in the system is a gorai service speaking the gorai-NATS protocol. The agent that reasons, the LLM behind the agent, the capability services that expose tools to the agent, the cameras and motors and sensors on a robot — all of them are services on the same mesh, declared in the same RDL, discovered by the same registry, started by the same lifecycle. Tools are operations a service exposes; services are the addressable units of the mesh.
37	
38	The layering looks like this:
39	
40	```
41	┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
42	│  Agents (services with reasoning loops)                  │
43	│   coding-planner   coding-coder   robot-navigator        │
44	│   robot-perception  fleet-coordinator   sensor-monitor   │
45	└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
46	           │  gorai-NATS protocol  (the common contract)  │
47	           ▼                                               ▼
48	  ┌─────────────────────┐                  ┌──────────────────────┐
49	  │ LLM adapter svcs    │                  │ Tool / component svcs│
50	  │  anthropic, ollama  │                  │ file, bash, motor,   │
51	  │  openai, vllm...    │                  │ lidar, camera, ...   │
52	  └─────────────────────┘                  └──────────────────────┘
53	           │                                               │
54	           ▼                                               ▼
55	   ┌──────────────┐                            ┌───────────────────┐
56	   │ LLM provider │                            │ OS, network,      │
57	   │ APIs         │                            │ filesystem,       │
58	   │ (Claude API, │                            │ hardware, sensors │
59	   │  Ollama,...) │                            │                   │
60	   └──────────────┘                            └───────────────────┘
61	```
62	
63	The roles in this layering:
64	
65	- **Agents** are services with a reasoning loop. They consume LLM completions, dispatch tool calls, hold conversation state, and decide when to stop. Multiple agents can run in the same mesh — each addressable by name, each potentially using a different LLM, each callable by other agents.
66	- **LLM adapter services** translate gorai-NATS on one side and a particular LLM's native protocol on the other. The Anthropic adapter speaks gorai-NATS to agents and the Anthropic Messages API to claude.ai. The Ollama adapter does the same for the local OpenAI-compatible endpoint. Each adapter handles its backend's specific quirks locally and presents a single canonical shape to its callers.
67	- **Capability services expose tools.** A `files` service exposes tools like `read_file`, `write_file`, `edit_file`. A `shell` service exposes `bash`. A `repo` service exposes `git_status`, `git_diff`. Each service is the mesh-level participant; the tools are what it advertises and accepts calls for. The pattern is the same on a robot: a motor service exposes `set_speed`, `read_position`; a camera service exposes `capture`, `get_info`. Tools are operations; services are the addressable units that host them.
68	- **Robot component services** — motors, cameras, sensors — are services exactly as they are in gorai today, exposing their own tool sets. The coding agent and the robot agent share the mesh; nothing prevents one from invoking the other.
69	
70	A coding agent doesn't know whether the LLM behind it is Claude, GPT-5, or qwen3-coder running on the laptop next to it. It publishes a completion request to an LLM adapter service by name; the adapter handles everything else. A robot navigation agent doesn't know whether the LiDAR feeding it is an RPLidar A2 or a Hokuyo; it subscribes to the LiDAR service's data subject and gets a canonical scan shape.
71	
72	The implications follow directly:
73	
74	- **Adding a new LLM** = writing a new adapter service. No agent code changes.
75	- **Adding a new tool** = adding it to an existing service, or writing a new service that exposes it. Either way, no agent code changes — agents discover tools from the mesh.
76	- **Adding a new sensor or actuator** = writing a new component service. The same agent can drive it.
77	- **Mixing roles** = composing services in the RDL. A coding agent that monitors a hardware sensor and writes code based on what it senses is just two existing services wired together.
78	
79	The gorai-NATS protocol becomes the public contract. Every service implements it; every service is interchangeable as long as it speaks the contract.
80	
81	---
82	
83	## Agent-to-Agent on the Mesh
84	
85	When agents collaborate — a planner producing a plan that a coder executes; a perception agent informing a navigation agent; a fleet coordinator dispatching missions to individual robots — they talk to each other over NATS. The pattern is the same one Google's Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol describes for distributed AI agents, but the transport is the gorai mesh rather than HTTP.
86	
87	An agent on the mesh has:
88	
89	- **A name** — its NATS subject prefix, e.g. `agent.planner-claude.*` or `agent.robot-1-navigator.*`.
90	- **A capability declaration** — published periodically on a status subject, naming what it can do, what LLM it uses, what tools it has access to.
91	- **A request subject** — where it accepts work from other agents.
92	- **A response subject** — where it publishes results.
93	- **A progress subject** — optional intermediate events (reasoning steps, partial outputs) for observers.
94	
95	A coordinator agent that runs a planner→coder→reviewer pipeline is just another agent that publishes work to those agents' request subjects and reads their responses. A robot fleet coordinator that runs a perception→planning→execution pipeline does the same thing with different agent names. The pattern is identical; only the participants differ.
96	
97	This makes several things free that would otherwise be infrastructure:
98	
99	- **Agents are addressable.** The RDL names which agent should handle which role. Swapping `planner-claude` for `planner-local` is a one-line edit. Swapping `navigator-default` for `navigator-aggressive` is the same edit.
100	- **Agents can be in different processes or on different machines.** The wire protocol does not change. A coordinator on a laptop can dispatch work to a coder running on a server, or to a specialized agent running next to a sensor on a robot.
101	- **Agents can have different implementations.** An agent written in Python for a model with Python-only inference support speaks the same gorai-NATS protocol. The mesh does not care what language a service is written in.
102	- **Observability is built in.** Every inter-agent message is on NATS, inspectable with `nats sub`. Replay is just re-publishing the captured messages.
103	
104	A2A-style coordination — the kind of thing Google designed an HTTP protocol for — is, in this architecture, the default communication pattern. There is no separate A2A layer; there is the mesh.
105	
106	---
107	
108	## Why MCP Becomes Redundant
109	
110	The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is the emerging standard for tools to be reusable across AI systems. An MCP server exposes a capability (a database, a search index, a sensor); an MCP-aware AI agent reads the capability advertisement and calls it.
111	
112	In a gorai-NATS mesh, this is just... a service. A database service subscribes to a NATS subject, advertises its capabilities on a status subject, accepts queries, returns results. Agents discover it via the mesh registry and call it like any other tool. No separate protocol layer is needed because the mesh already provides the things MCP provides: capability advertisement, addressable endpoints, request/response semantics, optional streaming.
113	
114	This is not a rejection of MCP — it is the same architectural idea, with the transport replaced by the mesh. If someone has an existing MCP server they want to expose to gorai agents, the bridge is itself a gorai service: speaks gorai-NATS on one side and MCP on the other. Same pattern as the LLM adapter services, applied to a different protocol.
115	
116	The implication: **we do not design a separate plugin or extension system for tools.** The mesh IS the extension system. New tool = new service, declared in the RDL, discovered at startup, invokable by any agent that knows its name. This was true for gorai's robot components from the start; it is true for our coding agent's tools as well; it will be true for any external capability we bridge in later.
117	
118	---
119	
120	## Skills: The Universal Behavior Language
121	
122	The most consequential idea in this vision is that **skills are not a coding-tool feature**. Skills — markdown files with YAML frontmatter describing when to apply them, what to do, and what success looks like — are a generic format for encoding agent behavior. The Anthropic / Claude Code community has spent the last two years building a rich ecosystem of skills for software work. That ecosystem turns out to be a behavior library that applies to *any* agent on the mesh.
123	
124	Consider three skills authored in the same format:
125	
126	**For a coding agent:**
127	```markdown
128	---
129	name: test-driven-development
130	description: Write the failing test, watch it fail, then write the code
131	triggers: [implement, fix bug, add feature]
132	permissions: standard
133	---
134	Before writing implementation code:
135	1. Write a test that captures the requirement or reproduces the bug.
136	2. Run it and confirm it fails for the expected reason.
137	3. Write the minimum code to make the test pass.
138	4. Run it and confirm it passes.
139	Never declare the work complete without showing a passing test.
140	```
141	
142	**For a robot navigation agent:**
143	```markdown
144	---
145	name: safe-doorway-traversal
146	description: Slow approach and re-scan before passing through doorways or narrow passages
147	triggers: [approaching doorway, narrow passage detected, threshold]
148	permissions: motion-with-safety-override
149	---
150	When perception identifies a doorway or narrow passage:
151	1. Reduce speed to 0.2 m/s before entering the threshold zone.
152	2. Re-scan with the primary LiDAR to verify clearance on both sides.
153	3. Halt if any obstacle is within 30 cm of the planned path.
154	4. Document the traversal in the .llm/traversals log.
155	Never proceed through a doorway at full nominal speed.
156	```
157	
158	**For a sensor-monitor agent:**
159	```markdown
160	---
161	name: degraded-battery-prioritization
162	description: Drop non-critical sensor sampling when battery falls below threshold
163	triggers: [battery low, power warning]
164	permissions: read-only-with-config-override
165	---
166	When battery falls below 20%:
167	1. Identify all non-critical sensor subscriptions (anything not safety-related).
168	2. Increase their sampling intervals by 4×.
169	3. Publish a degraded-mode notice on the fleet status subject.
170	4. Restore normal intervals when battery recovers above 30%.
171	```
172	
173	Three completely different domains. Same file format. Same loading semantics. Same triggering pattern. Same authoring tools.
174	
175	This matters because:
176	
177	- **Skills are authored by domain experts, not programmers.** A roboticist who knows safe doorway traversal does not need to know Go, Python, or any other language to encode that knowledge — they need to know markdown.
178	- **Skills are portable.** The TDD skill works for Claude Code today, goraic tomorrow, and any other agent that loads gorai-format skills.
179	- **Skills are versioned and reviewable.** They are text files. They go in git. They get pull requests. Domain knowledge becomes reviewable artifacts instead of tribal knowledge.
180	- **Skills are composable.** A robot agent in a hospital could load TDD (for any code it generates in the field), safe-doorway-traversal (for navigation), and HIPAA-aware-logging (for what it can record). The skill set is the agent's behavior set.
181	
182	The Anthropic ecosystem alignment isn't just about reusing models — it's about reusing the entire behavior-encoding language. Every skill written by every prompt engineer for Claude Code is a potential building block for a robot, a fleet coordinator, or a sensor mesh. This is the leverage that turns "AI behavior" from a per-vendor proprietary thing into a shared community asset.
183	
184	---
185	
186	## Local-First, Cloud When You Need It
187	
188	Modern consumer hardware carries the models to make local-first AI real. A machine with 48–128 GB of unified RAM can run Qwen3-Coder, Llama-3.3-70B-Q6, or other current open models at quality approaching what foundation labs charge for. For a robot, this means the autonomy can be on-board — air-gapped, latency-free, and free per inference. For a developer, this means the coding agent works offline, on an airplane, in a sensitive codebase that cannot leave the building.
189	
190	The architectural answer follows from the everything-is-a-service principle: **multiple LLM adapter services in the RDL, each backed by a different provider, addressed by name from the agents that use them.**
191	
192	- **Ollama adapter service**: Air-gapped, private, zero cost per token. Covers the high-volume, lower-complexity work. Speaks gorai-NATS to agents and Ollama's local OpenAI-compatible API to the model. Handles model-specific quirks (silent tool-call-as-text degradation, content-null vs empty-string, arguments double-encoding) so they never reach the agent.
193	- **Anthropic adapter service**: Network-connected, highest capability. Reserved for tasks that need it: complex multi-file reasoning, architecture planning, code review, novel debugging, high-stakes robotic planning. Speaks gorai-NATS to agents and the Anthropic Messages API to claude.ai.
194	- **Other adapters as needed**: OpenAI, vLLM for fine-tuned models, even mock adapters for testing. Each is a service. Each lives in the same mesh.
195	
196	Both adapter services appear in the same RDL. The agent's RDL entry names the adapter it should use — `llm: anthropic-claude-sonnet` or `llm: ollama-qwen3-coder`. Switching is a one-line edit; the agent code does not know or care which adapter is on the other end.
197	
198	A developer on an airplane runs only the Ollama adapter; their RDL points the agent at it. A robot in a remote location runs only its on-board adapter; its mission planner trusts whatever is on-board. The same developer, online, can route complex planning phases to the Anthropic adapter by naming it in the planner role and routing routine phases to the Ollama adapter by naming it in the coder role. Multiple LLM adapters running in the same mesh is the normal case, not a special case.
199	
200	This is also the architectural answer to a problem that surfaced in the first implementation pass: a single Go-interface provider abstraction had to lie about three structurally different LLM protocols, and silent failures (model emits tool calls as text content instead of structured calls) escaped detection. Moving the protocol translation into a per-LLM service makes each adapter's failure modes containable, testable, and visible on the bus.
201	
202	---
203	
204	## Multi-Agent as Architecture, Not Feature
205	
206	The single-agent loop — one LLM, one tool set, one session — is where most AI agents stop. It is also where they hit the limits of context windows, model capability, and task complexity. And it is fundamentally incompatible with physical AI, where perception, planning, and actuation are inherently parallel concerns.
207	
208	The right architecture is multi-agent from the start, and because everything is a service, "multi-agent" just means "more than one agent service in the RDL." For a coding mission, this is the planner→coder→reviewer pipeline. For a robot mission, this is perception→planning→execution. For a fleet, this is fleet-coordinator dispatching missions to individual robots, each of which is itself a multi-agent system.
209	
210	In every case, the shape is the same:
211	
212	- A **coordinator agent** holds the mission. It decomposes into phases, tracks state, and dispatches work to other agent services via A2A-on-NATS.
213	- One or more **role-specific agents** execute the phases. Each is a gorai service with its own LLM adapter, its own tool set, its own skill list.
214	- **Capability services** — exposing file, shell, repo, sensor, motor, and other tools — are called by the role agents over NATS.
215	- **LLM adapter services** handle the model-specific protocol translation invisibly.
216	
217	The implementation pattern is identical whether the role agents are `planner-claude` + `coder-qwen3` + `reviewer-claude`, or `perception-vision-model` + `planner-foundation-model` + `executor-control-loop`. The coordinator does not need to know what kind of agents it is dispatching to — it only needs the agent names from the RDL.
218	
219	A robot operator monitoring a field deployment and a developer watching a coding session are looking at the same kind of thing: a coordinator dispatching agents, agents reporting state, LLM adapters processing completion requests, the mesh reflecting the current plan and its execution. Observability tools, replay, and audit logs built for the robot case work for the coding case without modification — and vice versa.
220	
221	---
222	
223	## The Coding Agent: First Practical Example
224	
225	A vision this large needs a concrete, working proof point. The first build on this architecture is a coding agent — and that is a deliberate choice, not an accident.
226	
227	The coding agent is a good first example because:
228	
229	- **The use case is universal.** Every developer needs coding help. The audience is large, the value is immediate, the comparison points are clear (Claude Code, Cursor, Aider).
230	- **The architecture exercises every part of the mesh.** Reasoning loops, LLM adapter services, capability services exposing tools, skill loading, multi-agent coordination, A2A — all of them appear in their canonical form.
231	- **The failure modes are observable.** A coding agent that produces wrong code is immediately visible; the user can read the diff. This is much faster feedback than running a robot in a test field.
232	- **It bootstraps the ecosystem.** Skills written for the coding agent feed directly into the broader skill library that future robot agents and other domain agents will draw from.
233	
234	The refined vertical slice is **four services communicating over the gorai-NATS protocol**:
235	
236	1. **A coordinator agent service** — receives the mission from the user, decomposes into phases, dispatches phases to behavior agents by name.
237	2. **A coder agent service** — runs a ReAct loop, consumes LLM completions via its configured LLM adapter, invokes tools by discovering them from capability services on the mesh.
238	3. **An LLM adapter service** — speaks gorai-NATS to the coder and the LLM's native protocol to the model. The first implementations target Anthropic (cloud foundation model) and Ollama (local).
239	4. **Capability services** — `files`, `search`, `shell`, `repo`, `memory`. Each is a service that advertises the tools it exposes (`read_file`, `write_file`, `grep`, `bash`, etc.) and accepts invocations on its subject space. Tools are the operations; services are the addressable units that host them.
240	
241	A working version of this slice — coordinator dispatches one phase to coder, coder calls LLM adapter for a completion, coder calls into capability services to invoke tools and edit files, coder publishes result, coordinator surfaces it to user — proves the entire architecture. Multi-agent orchestration (more behaviors), multi-LLM routing (more adapters), tool extensions (more services or more tools on existing services), and eventually robot components are all additive. None of them require re-architecture.
242	
243	**Deployment in v0.1: a single binary, services as goroutines, embedded NATS.** The services above are conceptually separate — each owns a bounded NATS subject space, each could later be split into its own process — but in v0.1 they ship together. The embedded NATS server starts inside the binary; the agent, coordinator, LLM adapter, and capability services run as goroutines that publish and subscribe through it. There is no inter-process communication, no external NATS daemon, no separate adapter binaries. From the user's perspective, `goraic` is one executable that does the whole job.
244	
245	This is a deliberate choice. The architectural commitments — services on a mesh, gorai-NATS as the contract, LLM adapters that own their own protocol translation — are preserved in the code structure. The pragmatic concession is that everything lives in one process. The boundary between, say, the coder agent and the Anthropic adapter is a real NATS subject (publishing a `TaskRequest`, awaiting a `TaskResult`) — not a Go interface call. This makes the v0.1 binary easy to install and run, and at the same time keeps the door open: when distributing across processes or machines becomes worth it, the work is "give the adapter its own `main.go`," not "redesign the abstractions."
246	
247	What we built in the first implementation pass tried to honor the single-binary deployment but skipped the NATS contract internally — providers were a Go interface, called directly. The lesson from real-LLM testing was clear: the protocol differences between Anthropic, OpenAI/Ollama, and individual model quirks are too consequential to hide behind an in-process Go abstraction. Putting a real NATS-subject boundary between agents and LLM adapters — even when they live in the same process — is the refinement that makes the substrate sound. The v0.2 build preserves the simple single-binary deployment but moves the LLM call across that boundary.
248	
249	The coding agent, built this way, is the first practical example. It is also immediately useful on its own. But its real purpose is to prove that the architecture works — so that when the second example arrives (a robot perception agent, a fleet coordinator, a multi-modal sensor monitor), the substrate is ready.
250	
251	---
252	
253	## The User Interface: TUI, Not a Log Dump
254	
255	The gorai coding agent presents a **text user interface (TUI)** modeled after Claude Code and pi-go: a clean, interactive terminal experience that renders the agent's reasoning, tool calls, and outputs in structured panels — not a scrolling wall of log lines.
256	
257	This distinction matters architecturally. The terminal is a *collaboration surface*, not a debug console. When the agent is working, the user sees the agent's plan, the current phase, tool invocations as they happen, and the final result — not NATS envelope headers, LLM token counts, or service lifecycle messages.
258	
259	**Logging policy:** All internal gorai messages — service startup, NATS subscriptions, LLM request/response traces, error backtraces, timing telemetry — are written exclusively to a log file. The log file path is specified in the gorai configuration (e.g., `~/.config/gorai/config.json`). The default is a sensible platform path (e.g., `~/.local/share/gorai/gorai.log` on Linux). Nothing that is not intended for the user appears on stdout or the TUI surface.
260	
261	The separation enforces a clean contract:
262	
263	- **TUI / stdout** — user-facing content only: agent reasoning, tool results, questions, and final outputs.
264	- **Log file** — everything a developer needs to debug: all internal events, all NATS traffic, all LLM traces, all service state transitions.
265	
266	This is how Claude Code behaves: you interact with the agent, and when something goes wrong you look at a log file — not at the terminal scroll. Pi-go follows the same model. Gorai must too, from the first prototype forward. Retrofitting silent logging onto a tool that has always printed freely is painful; designing it in from the start is not.
267	
268	The TUI also keeps a **persistent status line** pinned to the bottom of the terminal — a spinner, the current action, and elapsed time — while finished output scrolls above it, exactly the way Claude Code and pi-go do. This is achieved with a terminal scroll region (DECSTBM), **not** the alternate screen: the transcript above the status line stays in ordinary scrollback and remains natively selectable and copyable. The status line is shown only during a live, interactive turn on a real terminal; in pipes or non-interactive runs it is suppressed.
269	
270	---
271	
272	## The Heterogeneous Mesh
273	
274	The endgame this architecture enables: a single gorai mesh hosting **agents from different domains, working together**.
275	
276	Consider a research lab that builds autonomous boats. On the mesh:
277	
278	- A **coding agent** running on a developer's laptop, writing new control algorithms based on field-test telemetry.
279	- A **robot perception agent** running on the boat, detecting buoys and obstacles.
280	- A **navigation planner agent** running on the boat, consuming perception output and producing waypoints.
281	- A **mission coordinator agent** running on a shore station, dispatching test missions to the boat and capturing telemetry.
282	- A **fleet observability agent** running in the cloud, watching all of the above, surfacing anomalies to the human operator.
283	
284	Every one of these is a gorai service. They share the same NATS mesh. They share the same skill library. The coding agent can read the perception agent's status to understand the failure modes it should code for. The mission coordinator can ask the coding agent to fix a bug surfaced during a field test. The fleet observability agent can dispatch a re-test mission once the coding agent reports the fix. Humans appear in the loop where they want to — but the agents do not require human bridges to communicate with each other.
285	
286	This is not a roadmap. It is the natural consequence of the architecture once one piece is built. Each agent that lands in the mesh makes the next one easier. Each skill written for one agent becomes available to all of them. Each LLM adapter added serves every agent that wants it.
287	
288	The bet is that this composition becomes more valuable than any individual agent — and that the platform delivering it becomes more valuable than any individual product built on it.
289	
290	---
291	
292	## Open Source and the Ecosystem Play
293	
294	This project is open source. The bet is that the AI agent community and the robotics community converge on the same infrastructure problems, and that a platform which serves both is more valuable than two separate platforms that serve neither well.
295	
296	The extension points are clear:
297	
298	- **Skills** — markdown files in the gorai skill format. Anyone can author one. A library curated by the community will, over time, cover the major behavior patterns across coding, robotics, automation, and beyond. Skills authored for Claude Code work as-is.
299	- **LLM adapter services** — one binary per supported backend. Add a new adapter, declare it in your RDL, and every agent on the mesh can use it.
300	- **Capability services** — one binary per capability domain. A `files` service for filesystem operations, a `shell` service for command execution, a `git` service for repo introspection, a `motor` service for robot control. Each service hosts one or more tools, advertises them on the mesh, and is independently authorable and shippable.
301	- **Agent services** — one binary per role. The community can publish reference implementations: `coder-tdd-strict`, `planner-architecture-first`, `reviewer-security-focused`, `navigator-aggressive`, `perception-multi-modal`.
302	- **Bridges** — MCP bridges, A2A bridges, ROS bridges. Each is itself a gorai service. The mesh becomes the universal join point for AI infrastructure that would otherwise stay siloed.
303	
304	Components follow gorai ecosystem conventions (`gorai-agent-*`, `gorai-skill-*`, `gorai-llm-*`, `gorai-tool-*`). The goal is not to compete with Claude Code for coding, or with ROS 2 for robotics. The goal is to give the AI agent and robotics communities the infrastructure they would build for themselves if they paused long enough to realize they have the same architectural needs.
305	
306	---
307	
308	*Build the coding agent. Prove the substrate. Then open the mesh to everything else — robots, fleets, sensors, automation, and whatever comes next.*
309
